Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: What is the legal distinction between an offer and an invitation to treat?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer:
An offer is a definite promise to be bound on acceptance of specific terms — it creates the power of acceptance in the offeree. An invitation to treat (ITT) is merely an invitation to make an offer; it creates no legal obligation.

**Key rule:** an offer + acceptance = contract; an ITT does not. Goods displayed on shelves are an ITT: Pharmaceutical Society v Boots [1953].
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: OFFER · INVITATION TO TREAT · DEFINITE PROMISE · ITT ≠ OFFER · BOOTS

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: A supermarket displays tins of soup on shelves with price labels. A customer places one in their basket. At what point is a contract formed?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer: No contract is formed when the customer places goods in the basket. The shelf display is an invitation to treat (Pharmaceutical Society v Boots [1953]). The customer makes the offer at the till; the cashier accepts on behalf of the supermarket. The contract is formed at the point of acceptance at the till — not before.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: SHELF = ITT · OFFER AT TILL · BOOTS · CONTRACT AT ACCEPTANCE

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: What is the general rule regarding advertisements and offers? What is the main exception?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer:
Advertisements are generally invitations to treat, not offers (Partridge v Crittenden [1968]). They invite the public to make offers.

**Exception:** an advertisement may constitute an offer where it is unilateral in nature and sufficiently certain in terms — e.g. a reward advertisement (Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893]): acceptance by performance, no notification of acceptance required.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: ADVERTISEMENT = ITT · PARTRIDGE v CRITTENDEN · EXCEPTION: UNILATERAL OFFER · CARLILL

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: In Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893], the defendant advertised £100 to anyone who used their product and still caught influenza. Why was this a binding offer, not an ITT?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer:
The advertisement was a unilateral offer:

1. it was sufficiently certain
2. addressed to the whole world
3. the company deposited £1,000 in a bank to demonstrate sincerity.

Mrs Carlill accepted by performing the act required (using the smoke ball as directed). No prior notification of acceptance was needed. Consideration was the act of use. The £100 reward was enforceable.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: CARLILL · UNILATERAL OFFER · ACCEPTANCE BY PERFORMANCE · CERTAINTY · SINCERITY

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: What is the legal status of a bid made at an auction, and what is the effect of an auction 'without reserve'?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer: A bid is an offer by the bidder. The auctioneer's acceptance (fall of the hammer) forms the contract. The auctioneer's call for bids is an ITT (Payne v Cave [1789]); a bidder may withdraw before the hammer falls. Auction 'without reserve': the auctioneer is obliged to sell to the highest bidder — this obligation is enforceable as a separate unilateral contract (Barry v Davies [2000]).
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: BID = OFFER · HAMMER = ACCEPTANCE · ITT · PAYNE v CAVE · WITHOUT RESERVE · BARRY v DAVIES

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: What is the legal status of a price list or catalogue sent to potential customers?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer: A price list or catalogue is an invitation to treat, not an offer (Grainger & Son v Gough [1896]). It invites customers to make offers to purchase. The seller is not obliged to sell at listed prices and may reject any order. This protects sellers from being unable to meet potentially unlimited demand.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: PRICE LIST = ITT · GRAINGER v GOUGH · SELLER NOT BOUND · PROTECTS SELLER

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: Classify each of the following as either an offer (O) or an invitation to treat (ITT): (a) goods displayed in a shop window; (b) an advertisement in a newspaper; (c) a bid at an auction; (d) an auctioneer calling for bids; (e) a completed tender submitted in response to an invitation to tender; (f) a website displaying goods with a 'Buy Now' button.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer: (a) ITT — Pharmaceutical Society v Boots [1953]; (b) ITT — Partridge v Crittenden [1968] (unless unilateral: Carlill); (c) Offer — Payne v Cave [1789]; (d) ITT — Payne v Cave; (e) Offer — the tenderer makes the offer; the invitor accepts. (f) Generally ITT — the contract is typically formed when the seller confirms the order, not at point of clicking 'Buy Now' (Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking by analogy; Regulation 11, E-Commerce Regulations 2002 may also be relevant). Apply: only (c) and (e) are offers — the rest are ITTs.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: O vs ITT · SHOP WINDOW ITT · AD ITT · BID = O · AUCTIONEER = ITT · TENDER = O · WEBSITE = ITT

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: What are the specific rules on revocation and performance in unilateral contracts, and how does Errington v Errington apply?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer:
A unilateral contract arises where one party (the offeror) promises something in exchange for the other party's act (not a promise). Classic example: 'I will pay you £100 if you walk to York.' Acceptance occurs on completion of the act — not before. Revocation before performance begins: the offeror can revoke at any time before the offeree starts performing — no contract yet exists. Revocation once performance has begun: the offeror cannot revoke once the offeree has embarked on performance of the requested act. Errington v Errington & Woods [1952]: a father promised his son and daughter-in-law that the house would be theirs if they paid off the mortgage instalments. He died; his widow sought possession. CA held the promise was a unilateral contract that could not be revoked while the couple continued paying. Daulia v Four Millbank Nominees [1978]: Goff LJ confirmed the principle — once the offeree has begun performance, an implied obligation prevents the offeror from revoking. Mere preparation to perform is insufficient — performance itself must have begun.

⚠️ Exam trap: identify whether the contract is bilateral or unilateral first. In bilateral contracts, acceptance is a promise; in unilateral, it is an act.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: UNILATERAL CONTRACT · REVOCATION · PERFORMANCE BEGUN · ERRINGTON v ERRINGTON · DAULIA · NO REVOCATION MID-PERFORMANCE · PREPARATION ≠ PERFORMANCE

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: Pharmaceutical Society of GB v Boots Cash Chemists [1953]
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.issue: Whether the display of goods on pharmacy shelves constituted an offer to sell, such that the sale was completed when a customer placed goods in their basket.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.rule: The display of goods on shelves is an invitation to treat, not an offer. The customer makes the offer at the till; the contract is formed when the cashier accepts that offer.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.application: Boots displayed prescription medicines on open shelves. The Pharmaceutical Society argued that the sale occurred when goods were placed in the basket, before a pharmacist was present — breaching the Pharmacy Act. The Court of Appeal held the sale was completed at the cash desk, where a pharmacist was present, satisfying the statutory requirement.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.conclusion: Shop displays are invitations to treat. The offer is made by the customer at the till and accepted by the cashier. This foundational rule protects sellers from being contractually bound by every display of goods.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: DISPLAY = ITT · OFFER AT TILL · CONTRACT AT ACCEPTANCE · PHARMACY ACT · FOUNDATIONAL ITT RULE

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893]
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.issue: Whether a newspaper advertisement offering £100 to anyone who used the smoke ball and still caught influenza constituted a binding contractual offer capable of acceptance by the public.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.rule: A unilateral offer addressed to the world is binding once accepted by performance of the required act. No prior notification of acceptance is required. Sincerity and certainty of the offer are key.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.application: The company advertised £100 for influenza sufferers who used their product correctly, and deposited £1,000 in a bank to demonstrate sincerity. Mrs Carlill used the ball as directed and caught influenza. The Court of Appeal held the advertisement was a unilateral offer — it was sufficiently certain, addressed to the world, and sincerity was demonstrated by the bank deposit. Acceptance occurred by performance; no notification was needed.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.conclusion: Binding contract. A unilateral offer is accepted by performance of the stipulated act. Consideration was the inconvenience of using the smoke ball. The £100 was enforceable.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: CARLILL · UNILATERAL OFFER · ACCEPTANCE BY PERFORMANCE · NO NOTIFICATION · CERTAINTY · SINCERITY · £100 ENFORCEABLE

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: Ahmed visits a hardware store and picks up a hammer priced at £15. At the till, the cashier tells him the price label is wrong and the hammer is actually £25. Ahmed insists he is entitled to buy it for £15. Is Ahmed correct?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer: No. The price label in the shop is an invitation to treat, not an offer (Pharmaceutical Society v Boots [1953]). Ahmed made the offer to purchase when he presented the hammer at the till. The cashier, on behalf of the store, has the right to accept or reject that offer — and may correct the price before accepting. No contract has been formed at £15. Ahmed can either agree to pay £25 or leave without purchasing. The store is not obliged to sell at the labelled price.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: PRICE LABEL = ITT · OFFER AT TILL · CASHIER MAY REFUSE · BOOTS · NO CONTRACT AT £15

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: ElectroMart posts on its website: 'Brand new 65-inch TV — only £199 (RRP £1,200). Limited stock.' Maria clicks 'Add to cart' and 'Confirm Order'. She receives an automated email saying 'Thank you for your order — we will be in touch shortly.' Two hours later, ElectroMart emails to say the price was a mistake and cancels the order. Maria claims a binding contract was formed at £199. Advise her.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer:
Maria's claim is unlikely to succeed. A website displaying goods with prices is generally an invitation to treat, not an offer (analogous to Pharmaceutical Society v Boots [1953]; Fisher v Bell [1961]). When Maria clicked 'Confirm Order', she made the offer to purchase. The automated 'thank you' email was an acknowledgment, not an acceptance — it did not confirm agreement to the specific terms (ElectroMart said 'we will be in touch'). No binding acceptance was communicated. Furthermore, even if a contract were formed, ElectroMart might argue common mistake as to price. Under E-Commerce Regulations 2002 (reg.11), the contract is concluded when the service provider acknowledges receipt — but this depends on the precise wording. On these facts, no binding contract at £199 was formed.

⚠️ E-commerce formation rules should be verified against current regulations.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: WEBSITE = ITT · CONFIRM ORDER = OFFER · ACKNOWLEDGMENT ≠ ACCEPTANCE · E-COMMERCE REGS ⚠️ · NO CONTRACT

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: A painting is put up for auction 'without reserve'. The highest bid is £800 from James. The auctioneer refuses to accept the bid, believing the painting is worth more. James claims he is entitled to the painting at £800. Advise him.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer: James has a strong argument. In a standard auction the auctioneer's call for bids is an ITT and each bid is an offer (Payne v Cave [1789]). However, an auction 'without reserve' creates a separate unilateral contract: the auctioneer promises to sell to the highest bona fide bidder without a minimum price (Barry v Davies [2000]). When James made the highest bid, he accepted that unilateral offer by performing the required act. The auctioneer is bound by this separate unilateral contract to accept the highest bid and cannot refuse without breaching it. James is entitled to damages for breach of the collateral unilateral contract — likely the difference between his bid (£800) and the market value of the painting.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: WITHOUT RESERVE · UNILATERAL CONTRACT · HIGHEST BID = ACCEPTANCE · BARRY v DAVIES · AUCTIONEER BOUND · DAMAGES

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: Marcus tells his niece Sofia: 'I'll transfer ownership of my car to you once you've driven it for 12 months without any accidents.' After 10 months, Marcus changes his mind and tells Sofia the offer is withdrawn. Sofia has had no accidents. Can Marcus revoke?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer:
No — Marcus cannot revoke. This is a unilateral contract: Marcus has promised the car in exchange for Sofia performing an act (driving accident-free for 12 months). Acceptance occurs on completion of the act, not before. However, the rule in Errington v Errington & Woods [1952] and Daulia v Four Millbank [1978] provides that once the offeree has embarked on performance of the requested act, the offeror cannot revoke. Sofia has been performing for 10 months — she is well into performance of the requested act. Marcus cannot withdraw mid-performance. An implied term prevents revocation once performance has begun (Daulia, Goff LJ). If Sofia completes the remaining 2 months without accidents, the contract is formed and she is entitled to the car. Marcus would be in breach if he refuses to transfer it.

**Note:** if Sofia had an accident in month 11, the condition would not be met and no contract would form — but the revocation point is separate from whether Sofia ultimately succeeds in completing performance.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: UNILATERAL CONTRACT · PERFORMANCE BEGUN · ERRINGTON · DAULIA · NO REVOCATION MID-PERFORMANCE · CAR TRANSFER

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front: Going deeper: Is the invitation to treat doctrine still fit for purpose in the age of online retail?
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.answer: The ITT doctrine, rooted in Pharmaceutical Society v Boots [1953] and Partridge v Crittenden [1968], treats shop displays and advertisements as invitations rather than offers — protecting sellers from being bound by every display of goods. In physical retail, this reflects commercial reality: stock may run out; prices may be mislabelled. However, the doctrine creates tension in e-commerce, where the customer's 'checkout' action closely resembles acceptance of a standing offer. The E-Commerce Regulations 2002 (implementing the E-Commerce Directive) introduce a statutory framework: under reg.11, the service provider must acknowledge receipt of an order promptly, but the moment of contract formation depends on the operator's terms. Many retailers now insert a 'dispatch confirmation' step to ensure no contract forms before they can check stock. Academic debate: Treitel argues the ITT rule is a sensible default that parties can contract out of; McKendrick suggests the binary offer/ITT distinction is too rigid for modern transactional contexts. The Law Commission has not proposed reform, but the Consumer Rights Act 2015 provides a separate layer of protection for consumers regardless of formation rules. The deeper question is whether the offer/ITT distinction does meaningful work in consumer contracts — or whether consumer protection legislation has effectively displaced its significance.

Offer vs Invitation to Treat.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A property developer promises a broker: 'Find me a buyer for the warehouse and I'll pay you £20,000 commission.' The broker spends three months identifying and approaching potential buyers. With a deal about to close, the developer withdraws the offer, arguing that no contract has formed yet because the sale is not complete.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.issue: The Trap: In a unilateral contract, acceptance occurs only on completion of the requested act — not before. The developer argues that because the sale is incomplete, no contract exists yet and the general rule (an offer can be revoked any time before acceptance) allows revocation.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.rule: The Reality: The developer cannot revoke. Once the broker embarked on performance of the requested act, an implied term prevents revocation (Errington v Errington & Woods [1952]; Daulia v Four Millbank Nominees [1978]). Three months of active work is clearly performance begun — not mere preparation.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The developer's argument is technically correct on one point — acceptance in a unilateral contract does occur on completion. But it is wrong on revocation. The general revocation rule is qualified once performance has begun. Allowing revocation at this stage would allow the offeror to exploit the offeree's efforts and then escape payment — precisely the situation the Errington principle prevents. The distinction is: revocation before performance begins is permitted; after performance has begun, it is not.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.conclusion: The Key Rule: Errington v Errington; Daulia v Four Millbank — in a unilateral contract, once the offeree has embarked on performance, the offeror cannot revoke. Mere preparation is insufficient — performance itself must have begun. Three months of active work satisfies this. Distinguish from bilateral contracts where the general revocation rule applies without qualification.
Offer vs Invitation to Treat.summary_line: UNILATERAL CONTRACT · REVOCATION AFTER PERFORMANCE BEGUN · ERRINGTON · DAULIA · PREPARATION ≠ PERFORMANCE · IMPLIED TERM PREVENTS REVOCATION

Acceptance.front: What are the requirements for a valid acceptance?
Acceptance.answer:
A valid acceptance must:

1. be unconditional — match the offer exactly (mirror image rule)
2. be communicated to the offeror (subject to the postal rule exception)
3. be made while the offer is still open (not after revocation, rejection, lapse, or death)
4. comply with any required method specified in the offer.

A purported acceptance introducing new terms is a counter-offer, not an acceptance (Hyde v Wrench [1840]).
Acceptance.summary_line: ACCEPTANCE · UNCONDITIONAL · COMMUNICATED · MIRROR IMAGE · COUNTER-OFFER DESTROYS

Acceptance.front: What is the postal rule and when does it apply?
Acceptance.answer:
The postal rule: acceptance is effective when the letter is posted — not when it is received — provided it is reasonable to use the post (Adams v Lindsell [1818]).

**Requirements:**

1. post must be a reasonable and contemplated mode of communication
2. the letter must be properly addressed and stamped
3. the rule can be excluded by the offeror requiring actual communication (Holwell Securities v Hughes [1974]: 'notice in writing' meant the rule was excluded).

The rule does not apply to: instantaneous communications (telex, fax, email — Entores v Miles Far East Corp [1955]; Brinkibon v Stahag Stahl [1983]); withdrawals of acceptance (these are ineffective — once posted, acceptance is complete).

⚠️ EXAM WARNING — Asymmetry: the postal rule applies to ACCEPTANCE ONLY. Revocation is NEVER effective on posting — it only takes effect when received by the offeree (Byrne v Van Tienhoven [1880]). This asymmetry is a deliberate SQE distractor: an offeror who posts a revocation letter cannot rely on it until it reaches the offeree. If the offeree posts acceptance before receiving the revocation, a binding contract is formed.
Acceptance.summary_line: POSTAL RULE · ACCEPTANCE ON POSTING · ADAMS v LINDSELL · HOLWELL EXCLUDED · INSTANTANEOUS RULE · ⚠️ REVOCATION ON RECEIPT ONLY · BYRNE v VAN TIENHOVEN · ASYMMETRY

Acceptance.front: What is the effect of a counter-offer on the original offer? How is it distinguished from a request for information?
Acceptance.answer: A counter-offer destroys the original offer — it cannot subsequently be accepted (Hyde v Wrench [1840]: offer to sell farm at £1,000 rejected; counter-offer at £950 made; original offer no longer open). A request for information, by contrast, does NOT destroy the original offer (Stevenson, Jacques & Co v McLean [1880]) — it merely enquires whether different terms are possible.
Acceptance.summary_line: COUNTER-OFFER DESTROYS OFFER · HYDE v WRENCH · INQUIRY ≠ COUNTER-OFFER · STEVENSON

Acceptance.front: When, if ever, can silence constitute acceptance?
Acceptance.answer: As a general rule, silence cannot constitute acceptance (Felthouse v Bindley [1862]). An offeror cannot impose a contract by stipulating that silence means acceptance. Narrow exception: silence may amount to acceptance where there is an established prior course of dealing under which silence was consistently treated as acceptance. The rule strongly protects offerees from unsolicited obligations.
Acceptance.summary_line: SILENCE ≠ ACCEPTANCE · FELTHOUSE v BINDLEY · EXCEPTION: COURSE OF DEALING

Acceptance.front: How and when can an offer be revoked — including the special rules for unilateral contracts?
Acceptance.answer:
General rule: an offer can be revoked at any time before acceptance (Routledge v Grant [1828]). Revocation must be communicated to the offeree — it is not effective until received (Byrne v Van Tienhoven [1880]). Communication may be through a reliable third party (Dickinson v Dodds [1876]: offeree learned from a mutual acquaintance that the house had been sold; revocation was effective). A promise to keep an offer open is NOT binding unless supported by consideration (option contract). Unilateral contracts — special rule: once the offeree has begun performance of the requested act, the offeror cannot revoke. In Errington v Errington & Woods [1952], a father promised his son and daughter-in-law the house if they paid off the mortgage; he could not revoke while they continued paying. Performance must have begun — mere preparation is insufficient. This principle was affirmed in Daulia v Four Millbank Nominees [1978]: once the offeree has embarked on performance, an implied term prevents revocation.

⚠️ Exam trap: do not apply the general revocation rule to a unilateral contract where the offeree has started performing — this is a frequent SQE scenario.
Acceptance.summary_line: REVOCATION · COMMUNICATED · BYRNE v VAN TIENHOVEN · DICKINSON v DODDS · UNILATERAL · ERRINGTON · PERFORMANCE BEGUN · DAULIA

Acceptance.front: In what circumstances does the postal rule NOT apply, and how may an offeror exclude it?
Acceptance.answer:
The postal rule does NOT apply where:

1. the offeror expressly requires actual communication of acceptance (Holwell Securities v Hughes [1974]: 'notice in writing to the intending vendor' — posting insufficient; actual receipt required)
2. it would be 'manifest inconvenience and absurdity' to apply it (Holwell, per Russell LJ)
3. the method of communication is instantaneous — telephone, fax, email, or telex (Entores v Miles Far East Corp [1955]; Brinkibon v Stahag Stahl [1983]): acceptance effective on receipt.

An offeror excludes the postal rule by specifying that acceptance must be received, not merely posted.
Acceptance.summary_line: POSTAL RULE EXCLUDED · EXPRESS REQUIREMENT · HOLWELL v HUGHES · INSTANTANEOUS = RECEIPT · ENTORES · BRINKIBON

Acceptance.front: When is a contract formed in electronic and online contracting — and what rules govern acceptance by email and website?
Acceptance.answer:
The general rule is that acceptance takes effect when and where it is received, not when sent — the postal rule does NOT apply to electronic communications (Entores v Miles Far East Corp [1955]: instantaneous communications rule; Brinkibon v Stahag Stahl [1983]: telex received at offeror's machine). Email and electronic acceptances follow the instantaneous rule: acceptance is complete when the message is received into the offeror's server/inbox, not when read. E-Commerce Regulations 2002 (implementing EU Directive 2000/31): reg.11 requires that where a consumer places an order electronically, the service provider must acknowledge receipt without undue delay. The order and acknowledgement are deemed received when accessible to the parties. Importantly, the Regulations do not prescribe when the contract is formed — this depends on the website's terms. Many retailers use a two-step process:

1. order = offer by customer
2. dispatch confirmation = acceptance by retailer (not the initial 'order received' acknowledgement).

Website advertisements: an invitation to treat (not an offer) — the customer makes the offer by clicking 'purchase' (consistent with Boots / ITT principles).

⚠️ Exam trap: a pricing error on a website does not bind the retailer if the website is an ITT — the retailer can decline the customer's offer.
Acceptance.summary_line: ELECTRONIC CONTRACTING · INSTANTANEOUS RULE · ENTORES · BRINKIBON · EMAIL ON RECEIPT · E-COMMERCE REGS 2002 · DISPATCH CONFIRMATION · WEBSITE = ITT

Acceptance.front: What is the distinction between a counter-offer and a request for information — and why does it matter for offer termination?
Acceptance.answer:
A counter-offer is a response that introduces new or different terms — it destroys the original offer, which can no longer be accepted (Hyde v Wrench [1840]). A request for information is a response that merely enquires about the terms without introducing new ones — it does NOT destroy the original offer, which remains open for acceptance. Stevenson, Jacques & Co v McLean [1880]: McLean offered iron at 40s net cash. Stevenson telegraphed: 'Please wire whether you would accept forty for delivery over two months, or if not, longest limit you would give.' McLean did not reply and sold to a third party, then purported to revoke. Stevenson then accepted the original offer. Held: Stevenson's telegram was a request for information, not a counter-offer — the original offer survived and was validly accepted. How to distinguish:

1. Does the response introduce new terms or conditions? → Counter-offer.
2. Does it merely ask whether the offeror would consider different terms? → Request for information.

⚠️ Exam trap: in a negotiation chain, a price enquiry does not kill the offer. Students who assume the offer died at the enquiry will answer incorrectly — the original offer remains open until revoked or lapsed.
Acceptance.summary_line: COUNTER-OFFER KILLS OFFER · HYDE v WRENCH · REQUEST FOR INFORMATION DOES NOT · STEVENSON v McLEAN · ORIGINAL OFFER SURVIVES · NEGOTIATION CHAIN TRAP

Acceptance.front: What is the key asymmetry between the postal rule for acceptance and the rule for revocation — and why does it matter?
Acceptance.answer:
Acceptance: effective on posting (Adams v Lindsell [1818]) — the contract is formed at the moment the letter of acceptance is posted, even if it arrives late or is lost in the post (Household Fire Insurance v Grant [1879]). The postal rule applies only where:

1. post is a reasonable/contemplated means
2. the rule has not been excluded by the offeror.

Revocation: effective only on receipt — a posted revocation is NOT effective until it is actually received by the offeree (Byrne v Van Tienhoven [1880]). The asymmetry: acceptance and revocation are governed by different rules. An offeror cannot rely on a posted revocation until it reaches the offeree. Consequence — the classic exam scenario: Day 1: Offeror posts an offer. Day 5: Offeror posts a revocation. Day 6: Offeree posts acceptance (having not yet received revocation). Day 7: Offeree receives revocation. Result: binding contract formed on Day 6 (acceptance effective on posting). The revocation received on Day 7 is too late.

⚠️ The postal rule NEVER applies to revocation — only to acceptance.
Acceptance.summary_line: ACCEPTANCE ON POSTING · ADAMS v LINDSELL · REVOCATION ON RECEIPT ONLY · BYRNE v VAN TIENHOVEN · ASYMMETRY · CONTRACT FORMED BEFORE REVOCATION RECEIVED

Acceptance.front: Adams v Lindsell [1818]
Acceptance.issue: Whether a contract was formed when the claimants posted their acceptance, even before it had been received by the offerors.
Acceptance.rule: The postal rule: acceptance is complete and takes effect at the moment a letter of acceptance is posted — not when it is received by the offeror.
Acceptance.application: Defendants posted an offer to sell wool and misdirected it, causing delay. Claimants posted acceptance but defendants, having heard nothing, had already sold the wool to a third party. The court held that the contract was formed when the claimants posted their letter of acceptance — the postal rule applied as posting was the authorised means of acceptance.
Acceptance.conclusion: Contract formed on posting. The postal rule established. The offeror takes the risk of delay in the post where post is the authorised or reasonable means of acceptance.
Acceptance.summary_line: ADAMS v LINDSELL · POSTAL RULE · ACCEPTANCE ON POSTING · NOT ON RECEIPT · AUTHORISED MEANS · OFFEROR BEARS RISK

Acceptance.front: Hyde v Wrench [1840]
Acceptance.issue: Whether a counter-offer destroys the original offer so that it cannot subsequently be accepted.
Acceptance.rule: A counter-offer operates as a rejection of the original offer and destroys it entirely. The original offer cannot subsequently be revived or accepted.
Acceptance.application: Wrench offered to sell his farm for £1,000. Hyde replied offering £950 — a counter-offer. Wrench rejected that. Hyde then purported to accept the original £1,000 offer. The court held that the counter-offer at £950 had destroyed Wrench's original offer. No contract was formed at £1,000.
Acceptance.conclusion: Counter-offer = rejection and destruction of original offer. Mirror image rule: acceptance must match the offer exactly. Any qualification or change creates a new counter-offer.
Acceptance.summary_line: HYDE v WRENCH · COUNTER-OFFER = REJECTION · DESTROYS ORIGINAL · MIRROR IMAGE RULE · NO CONTRACT

Acceptance.front: Felthouse v Bindley [1862]
Acceptance.issue: Whether silence can constitute acceptance, and whether an offeror can impose acceptance by stipulating that no reply means agreement.
Acceptance.rule: Silence cannot constitute acceptance. An offeror cannot unilaterally impose a contract on the offeree by stipulating that silence or inaction will be treated as acceptance.
Acceptance.application: An uncle wrote to his nephew offering to buy a horse, adding 'if I hear no more about him, I consider the horse mine at £30 15s'. The nephew intended to accept but said nothing. The horse was accidentally sold at auction. No contract existed between uncle and nephew — the nephew's subjective intention was not communicated and silence was not acceptance.
Acceptance.conclusion: Acceptance must be communicated. Silence ≠ acceptance. An offeror cannot impose a contract by deeming silence acceptance — this protects offerees from unsolicited obligations.
Acceptance.summary_line: FELTHOUSE v BINDLEY · SILENCE ≠ ACCEPTANCE · CANNOT IMPOSE · COMMUNICATION REQUIRED · SUBJECTIVE INTENT INSUFFICIENT

Acceptance.front: Byrne & Co v Van Tienhoven & Co [1880]
Acceptance.issue: Whether a revocation of an offer takes effect when posted by the offeror, or only when received by the offeree.
Acceptance.rule: Revocation of an offer is only effective when communicated to and received by the offeree. The postal rule does not apply to revocations — they are not effective on posting.
Acceptance.application: Defendants posted revocation of their offer on 8 October. Claimants, unaware of the revocation, posted acceptance on 11 October and confirmed by telegram. Defendants received on 20 October. The court held that the contract was formed when the acceptance was posted on 11 October. The revocation, posted before acceptance but received after, was ineffective.
Acceptance.conclusion: Revocation effective only on receipt by offeree. The postal rule applies to acceptances but not revocations — the offeror must ensure actual communication of the withdrawal.
Acceptance.summary_line: BYRNE v VAN TIENHOVEN · REVOCATION ON RECEIPT · POSTAL RULE ≠ REVOCATION · CONTRACT FORMED ON POSTING ACCEPTANCE

Acceptance.front: Holwell Securities v Hughes [1974]
Acceptance.issue: Whether the postal rule applied where an option to purchase land required 'notice in writing to the intending vendor'.
Acceptance.rule: The postal rule can be excluded by the offeror. Where the offer expressly requires actual communication or receipt of acceptance, posting is insufficient — actual notice must be received. The rule also does not apply where its application would produce 'manifest inconvenience and absurdity'.
Acceptance.application: Hughes granted Holwell an option to purchase land, exercisable 'by notice in writing to the intending vendor'. Holwell posted the notice but it was never received. The Court of Appeal held that 'notice' required actual communication — the postal rule was excluded by the express terms of the option. The option had not been validly exercised.
Acceptance.conclusion: Postal rule excluded by express requirement of actual notice. Offerors can contract out of the postal rule. 'Notice' = actual receipt, not mere posting.
Acceptance.summary_line: HOLWELL v HUGHES · POSTAL RULE EXCLUDED · NOTICE = ACTUAL RECEIPT · EXPRESS TERMS · MANIFEST INCONVENIENCE

Acceptance.front: On Monday, Sanjay posts an offer to sell his car to Priya for £8,000, saying she has until Friday. On Wednesday, Priya posts a letter of acceptance. On Thursday, Sanjay — having heard nothing — posts a revocation. Priya's acceptance arrives on Friday; Sanjay's revocation arrives on Friday afternoon. Is there a binding contract?
Acceptance.answer: Yes — a binding contract was formed on Wednesday when Priya posted her acceptance. Under the postal rule (Adams v Lindsell [1818]), acceptance is complete on posting, not on receipt. Priya's letter of acceptance was posted on Wednesday, before Sanjay's revocation was posted on Thursday. A revocation is only effective when received by the offeree (Byrne v Van Tienhoven [1880]); the postal rule does not apply to revocations. Since the contract was already formed on Wednesday (on posting), Sanjay's revocation — posted after acceptance — was too late. The revocation arriving after the acceptance letter is irrelevant. Sanjay is bound.
Acceptance.summary_line: POSTAL RULE · ACCEPTANCE ON POSTING · WEDNESDAY · REVOCATION AFTER ACCEPTANCE · BYRNE v VAN TIENHOVEN · BOUND

Acceptance.front: Tara offers to sell her antique clock to David for £500, stating 'acceptance must be received by me by 5pm on Friday.' David posts a letter of acceptance on Thursday. It arrives Saturday. David claims a contract was formed on posting. Is he right?
Acceptance.answer: No. Tara's offer expressly required acceptance to be received by 5pm on Friday — she has contracted out of the postal rule. Where an offeror stipulates that acceptance must be received (not merely posted), the postal rule is excluded (Holwell Securities v Hughes [1974]: 'notice in writing to the intending vendor' required actual receipt). David's letter, arriving on Saturday, was not received by the stipulated deadline. No contract was formed. An offeror can always exclude the postal rule by specifying that acceptance must reach them. David's only option is to telephone or email an acceptance before 5pm Friday — but only if those methods are authorised by the offer.
Acceptance.summary_line: POSTAL RULE EXCLUDED · EXPRESS RECEIPT REQUIREMENT · HOLWELL v HUGHES · LATE ARRIVAL · NO CONTRACT

Acceptance.front: Kevin offers to sell his bicycle to Lucy for £200. Lucy replies: 'I'll take it — but can you include the lock as well?' Kevin says no. Lucy then says: 'OK, I accept your original offer of £200 for the bike alone.' Kevin refuses to sell, saying the original offer no longer stands. Who is right?
Acceptance.answer:
Kevin is correct. Lucy's first reply — 'I'll take it, but can you include the lock' — was a counter-offer, not a pure acceptance. It introduced a new term (inclusion of the lock) and thereby destroyed Kevin's original offer (Hyde v Wrench [1840]). Once a counter-offer is made, the original offer is extinguished and cannot be revived. Lucy's subsequent attempt to accept the original terms came too late — there was no offer left to accept.

**Note:** if Lucy had instead asked 'would you include the lock?' without conditioning her acceptance on it, this would have been a mere request for information (not a counter-offer: Stevenson, Jacques v McLean [1880]) and the original offer would have remained open.
Acceptance.summary_line: COUNTER-OFFER = REJECTION · HYDE v WRENCH · DESTROYS ORIGINAL · CANNOT REVIVE · INQUIRY vs COUNTER-OFFER

Acceptance.front: At 9am, Hina emails an offer to buy software licences. At 11am, TechCo's automated system sends a 'Thank you for your order' email. At 2pm, TechCo emails again saying they cannot fulfil the order due to a pricing error. Is there a binding contract?
Acceptance.answer: The answer depends on when and whether TechCo's acceptance was communicated. First, characterise the parties' actions: Hina's email is an offer to purchase. TechCo's website/order system is an invitation to treat — not an offer — so Hina made the offer. Is the 11am automated email an acceptance? This turns on TechCo's terms and the objective interpretation of the message. Under the E-Commerce Regulations 2002 (reg.11), an acknowledgement of receipt of an order does not automatically constitute acceptance — many retailers use a two-step process where only a dispatch/fulfilment confirmation constitutes acceptance. A 'thank you for your order' message is typically an acknowledgement, not acceptance. If TechCo's terms state that the contract is only formed on a specific confirmation (e.g. dispatch), no contract exists at 11am. If the 11am email is construed as acceptance: the contract was formed at 11am when the email was received by Hina (instantaneous communications rule — Entores v Miles Far East Corp [1955]). TechCo cannot unilaterally withdraw at 2pm. On the balance of facts: no contract is likely formed — the automated email is an acknowledgement, not a fulfilment confirmation.
Acceptance.summary_line: ELECTRONIC CONTRACTING · ITT WEBSITE · E-COMMERCE REGS 2002 · ACKNOWLEDGEMENT ≠ ACCEPTANCE · INSTANTANEOUS RULE ENTORES · PRICING ERROR

Acceptance.front: On Monday, Raj offers to sell his vintage motorcycle to Priya for £8,000, open until Friday. On Tuesday, Priya emails: 'Would you consider £7,500, or could you hold it until next month?' Raj does not reply. On Thursday, Raj sells the motorcycle to a third party. On Friday morning, Priya emails accepting the original £8,000 offer. Is there a binding contract?
Acceptance.answer: No binding contract — but the analysis of Priya's Tuesday email is important. Was Priya's Tuesday email a counter-offer or a request for information? Applying Stevenson, Jacques & Co v McLean [1880]: Priya did not reject the offer or introduce new definitive terms — she asked whether Raj would consider a lower price and whether he could extend the time. This is a request for information, not a counter-offer. The original £8,000 offer therefore survived Priya's Tuesday email — it was not destroyed. However, Raj sold the motorcycle to a third party on Thursday. Under Dickinson v Dodds [1876], revocation can be communicated through a reliable third party. The question is whether Priya knew of the sale before accepting on Friday. On these facts, there is no indication Priya knew — so whether the revocation was effective depends on whether she had actual notice of the sale. If she did not know: the acceptance on Friday may be valid and a contract formed — but Raj cannot perform (having sold to a third party), making him liable in damages. If she did know: the offer was effectively revoked before acceptance and no contract was formed. Key point: Priya's Tuesday email did NOT kill the offer — this is the central exam trap the question targets.
Acceptance.summary_line: STEVENSON v McLEAN · REQUEST FOR INFORMATION · OFFER SURVIVES · DICKINSON v DODDS · REVOCATION VIA THIRD PARTY · ACCEPTANCE TIMING

Acceptance.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Oliver emails Sophie an offer to buy her car for £5,000, stating: 'Please email me your acceptance by Friday.' Sophie, preferring to be formal, posts a letter of acceptance on Thursday. The letter arrives on Saturday. Oliver claims no contract was formed.
Acceptance.issue: The Trap: Sophie posted her acceptance before the Friday deadline. The postal rule (Adams v Lindsell) provides that acceptance is effective on posting. Students apply the rule mechanically: posted on Thursday → accepted before the deadline → binding contract.
Acceptance.rule: The Reality: No contract. The postal rule does not apply. Oliver's offer specified email as the required means of acceptance. By specifying a particular method, the offeror ousted the postal rule. Acceptance must comply with the required method — posting is not compliant. Holwell Securities v Hughes [1974]: 'notice in writing to the intending vendor' meant the rule was excluded; actual communication was required.
Acceptance.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The postal rule is a default that operates only where post is a reasonable and contemplated means of communication and the offeror has not excluded it. Where the offeror specifies a different method (here, email), the offeree must comply with that method — posting is irrelevant regardless of timing. The deadline of Friday is a red herring: even if Sophie had emailed on Thursday, that would be valid. But posting on Thursday is not valid acceptance at all, whenever it was sent.
Acceptance.conclusion: The Key Rule: The offeror can oust the postal rule by specifying the required method of acceptance. If a particular method is required, only acceptance by that method is effective. The postal rule is a default, not a trump card — it yields to any contrary indication in the offer.
Acceptance.summary_line: POSTAL RULE OUSTED · SPECIFIED METHOD · HOLWELL · EMAIL REQUIRED · POSTING NOT VALID ACCEPTANCE

Acceptance.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Raj offers to sell his motorcycle to Priya for £8,000. Priya replies: 'Would you consider £7,500, or could you hold it until next month?' Raj does not reply. The next day, Priya emails: 'I accept your offer of £8,000.' Raj says the original offer is no longer open.
Acceptance.issue: The Trap: Priya mentioned a lower price — £7,500. Students see a different figure and classify this as a counter-offer under Hyde v Wrench [1840], which destroys the original offer. If the offer is dead, Priya cannot accept it.
Acceptance.rule: The Reality: Priya's first message was a request for information, not a counter-offer. Under Stevenson, Jacques & Co v McLean [1880], enquiring whether the seller would consider different terms does not destroy the original offer. Priya's acceptance of £8,000 is valid — a binding contract was formed.
Acceptance.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The distinction turns on the objective meaning of the response. A counter-offer introduces definitive new terms ('I will pay £7,500'). A request for information merely asks whether the offeror would consider alternatives. Priya used exploratory language: 'Would you consider...?' — she was asking, not proposing. Hyde v Wrench applies to definitive counter-proposals, not enquiries. The mention of £7,500 in a conditional question is a red herring — it did not constitute an offer or a rejection of the existing offer.
Acceptance.conclusion: The Key Rule: Stevenson v McLean — 'Would you consider...?' is a request for information that leaves the original offer intact. Only a definitive proposal of different terms (Hyde v Wrench) constitutes a counter-offer that destroys the original. The presence of a different figure in an exploratory question does not make it a counter-offer.
Acceptance.summary_line: REQUEST FOR INFORMATION · STEVENSON v McLEAN · OFFER SURVIVES · HYDE v WRENCH · CONDITIONAL LANGUAGE ≠ COUNTER-OFFER

Battle of the Forms.front: What is the 'battle of the forms' and how do courts resolve it?
Battle of the Forms.answer: The battle of the forms arises where parties exchange conflicting standard-form terms and it is unclear which set governs the contract. Courts apply offer and acceptance principles: each set of terms sent is a counter-offer, destroying any earlier offer. The general rule is the 'last shot' doctrine — the party who fires the last set of terms before performance begins, without objection from the other side, has their terms accepted by conduct (Butler Machine Tool Co v Ex-Cell-O Corp [1979], CA). Courts may also find: (a) no contract was formed; (b) a 'reasonable' set of terms applies where conduct clearly indicates a contract was intended.
Battle of the Forms.summary_line: BATTLE OF FORMS · COUNTER-OFFER · LAST SHOT RULE · BUTLER MACHINE TOOL · ACCEPTANCE BY CONDUCT

Battle of the Forms.front: Butler Machine Tool Co v Ex-Cell-O Corp [1979]
Battle of the Forms.issue: Which party's standard terms governed a contract formed by an exchange of conflicting standard-form documents.
Battle of the Forms.rule: The battle of the forms is resolved by applying standard offer and acceptance analysis. The 'last shot' doctrine applies: the party whose terms are sent last, and accepted without objection by conduct, wins.
Battle of the Forms.application: Butler (seller) sent a quotation on terms including a price variation clause. Ex-Cell-O (buyer) responded with its own order form on different terms (no price variation clause), with a tear-off slip acknowledging the buyer's terms. Butler signed and returned the slip. The Court of Appeal held that Ex-Cell-O's order was a counter-offer; Butler's return of the signed slip was acceptance of the buyer's terms. The price variation clause did not apply.
Battle of the Forms.conclusion: Last shot rule confirmed. The party who fires the last set of terms before performance, which the other side accepts without objection (here by signing the slip), has their terms incorporated.
Battle of the Forms.summary_line: BUTLER MACHINE TOOL · BATTLE OF FORMS · LAST SHOT · COUNTER-OFFER · ACCEPTANCE BY CONDUCT · TEAR-OFF SLIP

Battle of the Forms.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

BuildCo sends a purchase order for steel beams on their standard terms, which include a clause stating: 'These terms shall be the master terms governing any contract between the parties.' SteelCo replies with an acknowledgement on their own standard terms. BuildCo accepts the delivered steel without any objection. A dispute arises. Whose terms govern?
Battle of the Forms.issue: The Trap: BuildCo's purchase order contains an explicit 'master terms' clause. Students treat this as a contractual term that survives the exchange — if the parties have already agreed that BuildCo's terms govern, surely that clause controls the outcome?
Battle of the Forms.rule: The Reality: SteelCo's terms govern. English law follows the 'last shot' rule (Butler Machine Tool Co v Ex-Cell-O Corp [1979]). The contract is formed on the terms of the party who fired the last document before performance. SteelCo's acknowledgement was the last shot; BuildCo accepted those terms by conduct when they took delivery without objection.
Battle of the Forms.application: Why the Distractor Fails: A 'master terms' clause in BuildCo's offer is merely a term of that offer. SteelCo's counter-offer on different terms destroyed BuildCo's offer — including the master terms clause within it. The original offer, and every clause in it, ceases to exist once a counter-offer is made. What survives is SteelCo's counter-offer, accepted by BuildCo's conduct in taking delivery. The master terms clause never became part of any binding contract.
Battle of the Forms.conclusion: The Key Rule: Butler Machine Tool — the last document before performance governs. A master terms clause in an earlier document cannot survive a counter-offer. Acceptance by conduct (taking delivery without objection) binds the accepting party to the last-shot terms. The sequence of documents, not any individual clause, determines whose terms apply.
Battle of the Forms.summary_line: LAST SHOT RULE · BUTLER MACHINE TOOL · MASTER TERMS CLAUSE DESTROYED · COUNTER-OFFER · ACCEPTANCE BY CONDUCT

Subject to Contract.front: What is the effect of the label 'subject to contract' on the formation of a contract?
Subject to Contract.answer:
The label 'subject to contract' generally prevents a binding contract forming until formal contracts are exchanged (Winn v Bull [1877]; Chillingworth v Esche [1924]). However, the courts recognise three distinct categories:

1. No contract until formal document signed: the parties intend to be bound only on exchange of formal contracts — the most common position in property transactions. Neither party is bound beforehand; either may withdraw without liability.
2. Immediately binding contract, to be formalised: the parties are immediately bound but agree to replace their agreement with a more formal document. The subsequent document does not create the contract — it records one already made (Branca v Cobarro [1947]: 'provisional agreement' was immediately binding).
3. Binding contract conditional on a future event: a contract is formed but performance is conditional on a specified event (e.g. obtaining planning permission).

⚠️ Exam trap: the SQE tests category
2. — the presumption that 'subject to contract' prevents formation is rebuttable.

If the parties' conduct shows they intended to be immediately bound, the label will not prevent a contract (RTS Flexible Systems v Molkerei Alois Müller [2010] SC).
Subject to Contract.summary_line: SUBJECT TO CONTRACT · THREE CATEGORIES · NO CONTRACT YET · IMMEDIATELY BINDING · CONDITIONAL · BRANCA v COBARRO · RTS v MÜLLER

Subject to Contract.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

After weeks of negotiation, Niamh agrees to buy a flat from a developer. They exchange emails confirming the price, completion date, and all fixtures. Niamh's final email states: 'Agreed — subject to contract.' The developer then withdraws and accepts a higher offer from another buyer. Niamh claims a binding contract exists because all terms were agreed.
Subject to Contract.issue: The Trap: All the essential terms are agreed — price, date, fixtures. There is clear offer, apparent acceptance, and complete agreement. Students apply the general rule that a contract is formed when all material terms are agreed, and conclude the 'subject to contract' label is merely a formality that cannot defeat an otherwise complete agreement.
Subject to Contract.rule: The Reality: No binding contract. The label 'subject to contract' prevents a contract forming until formal contracts are exchanged (Winn v Bull [1877]; Chillingworth v Esche [1924]). The presumption is strong — courts are very reluctant to find a binding contract despite the label. The developer is free to withdraw.
Subject to Contract.application: Why the Distractor Fails: Agreement on all terms is a necessary but not sufficient condition for contract formation. Parties may agree all terms and yet signal that they do not intend to be bound until a formal document is executed — and 'subject to contract' is exactly that signal. Critically, it was Niamh herself who used the phrase in her final email. She cannot now argue the label is a technicality. The developer's freedom to withdraw is exactly what 'subject to contract' is designed to preserve. RTS Flexible Systems v Molkerei [2010]: the presumption can be rebutted by clear conduct showing immediate binding intent — but there is nothing on these facts.
Subject to Contract.conclusion: The Key Rule: Winn v Bull — 'subject to contract' prevents binding contract formation until formal contracts are exchanged. Agreement on all commercial terms does not override a clear 'subject to contract' qualification. The label is particularly significant where it appears in the claimant's own correspondence. Both parties remain free to withdraw.
Subject to Contract.summary_line: SUBJECT TO CONTRACT · WINN v BULL · CHILLINGWORTH · ALL TERMS AGREED ≠ BINDING · NIAMH'S OWN LABEL · RTS FLEXIBLE EXCEPTION

Consideration.front: What is consideration and what are the essential rules governing it?
Consideration.answer:
Consideration is 'some right, interest, profit or benefit accruing to one party, or some forbearance, detriment, loss or responsibility given, suffered or undertaken by the other' (Currie v Misa [1875]). Essential rules:

1. must move from the promisee (Tweddle v Atkinson [1861])
2. must not be past (Re McArdle [1951])
3. must be sufficient (have some value in law) but need not be adequate
4. must not be illegal.
Consideration.summary_line: CONSIDERATION · MOVE FROM PROMISEE · NOT PAST · SUFFICIENT NOT ADEQUATE · CURRIE v MISA

Consideration.front: What is the rule on past consideration and what is the exception from Pao On v Lau Yiu Long?
Consideration.answer:
Past consideration is no consideration — an act already performed before a promise is made cannot be consideration for that promise (Re McArdle [1951]; Roscorla v Thomas [1842]). Exception (Lampleigh v Brathwait principle, confirmed in Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980]): past acts are valid consideration if:

1. done at the promisor's request
2. both parties understood payment would be made; and
3. the subsequent promise merely fixes the amount.
Consideration.summary_line: PAST CONSIDERATION = NONE · RE McARDLE · EXCEPTION: REQUEST + UNDERSTANDING · PAO ON

Consideration.front: Can performance of an existing public duty or contractual duty constitute good consideration?
Consideration.answer:
Existing public duty: generally no (Collins v Godefroy [1831]). But doing more than the public duty requires IS good consideration (Glasbrook Bros v Glamorgan CC [1925]). Existing contractual duty owed to the promisor: traditionally no (Stilk v Myrick [1809]). But Williams v Roffey Bros [1991] held that a promise to perform an existing duty may be good consideration if the promisor obtains a practical benefit, and the promise was not extracted by economic duress.

**Note:** Williams v Roffey has NOT been extended to part-payment of debts (Re Selectmove [1995]).

⚠️ Link — Economic Duress (see CON-040): a 'practical benefit' is only valid consideration if it was given freely. If the practical benefit was secured by a threat to breach the contract, the promise is voidable for economic duress (Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980]). The consideration and duress analyses must both be checked.
Consideration.summary_line: PUBLIC DUTY ≠ CONSIDERATION · GLASBROOK EXCEPTION · STILK v MYRICK · WILLIAMS v ROFFEY · PRACTICAL BENEFIT

Consideration.front: What is the rule in Pinnel's Case and what are the recognised exceptions?
Consideration.answer:
Pinnel's Case [1602] (affirmed in Foakes v Beer [1884]): payment of a lesser sum cannot satisfy a debt for a greater sum, even if the creditor accepts it.

**Exceptions:**

1. payment made earlier than due
2. payment in a different form (e.g. a chattel)
3. payment at a different place at creditor's request
4. promissory estoppel (High Trees)
5. part payment by a third party (Hirachand Punamchand v Temple [1911])
6. composition with creditors.
Consideration.summary_line: PINNEL'S CASE · LESSER SUM · FOAKES v BEER · EXCEPTIONS: EARLIER · DIFFERENT FORM · ESTOPPEL

Consideration.front: What is promissory estoppel and what are its requirements and limits?
Consideration.answer:
Promissory estoppel (Central London Property Trust v High Trees [1947]) prevents a party going back on a promise to waive strict legal rights where:

1. existing legal relationship
2. clear and unambiguous promise not to enforce rights
3. promisee relied on the promise
4. it would be inequitable to allow the promisor to resile.

**Key limits:** it is a shield not a sword — cannot found an independent cause of action (Combe v Combe [1951]). It may be suspensory (i.e. promisor can give notice to resume strict rights) rather than extinctive.
Consideration.summary_line: PROMISSORY ESTOPPEL · HIGH TREES · RELIANCE · INEQUITABLE · SHIELD NOT SWORD · SUSPENSORY

Consideration.front: Is consideration sufficient in each of the following? (a) A peppercorn rent; (b) a promise to refrain from a lawful act; (c) a natural love and affection; (d) performance of a pre-existing contractual duty owed to a third party.
Consideration.answer: (a) Yes — a peppercorn is sufficient consideration even if of no real value; courts do not question adequacy (Thomas v Thomas [1842]). (b) Yes — forbearance from a lawful act is good consideration (Hamer v Sidway [1891, US, persuasive]); refraining from something one is entitled to do has legal value. (c) No — natural love and affection is not good consideration in English law (Eastwood v Kenyon [1840]); it has no economic value in law. (d) Yes — performance of a duty owed to a third party IS good consideration for a promise by a different party (Shadwell v Shadwell [1860]; Scotson v Pegg [1861]). Distinguishable from the Stilk v Myrick rule which only prevents existing duties owed to the promisor being used as consideration.
Consideration.summary_line: PEPPERCORN = SUFFICIENT · FORBEARANCE = SUFFICIENT · LOVE ≠ CONSIDERATION · THIRD PARTY DUTY = SUFFICIENT · SHADWELL

Consideration.front: Why did the Court of Appeal in Re Selectmove Ltd [1995] refuse to extend Williams v Roffey Bros [1991] to part-payment of a debt, and what is the significance of this for the rule in Pinnel's Case?
Consideration.answer: In Williams v Roffey, the Court of Appeal held that a practical benefit to the promisor could constitute good consideration for a promise to pay more to complete an existing contract. In Re Selectmove, the Crown agreed to accept tax arrears by instalments. The CA refused to apply Williams v Roffey: the practical benefit argument, if applied to debt cases, would effectively overturn the rule in Pinnel's Case (affirmed by the House of Lords in Foakes v Beer [1884]) — a decision it was not open to the Court of Appeal to make. Significance: Williams v Roffey is confined to cases of additional payment for goods/services; it cannot be used to make part-payment of a debt good consideration for discharge of the full debt. Promissory estoppel (High Trees) remains the only viable route to enforcement in debt-reduction cases.
Consideration.summary_line: RE SELECTMOVE · WILLIAMS v ROFFEY NOT EXTENDED TO DEBT · PINNEL'S CASE PRESERVED · FOAKES v BEER · ESTOPPEL ONLY ROUTE

Consideration.front: A landlord promises a struggling tenant that they will accept half the contractual rent during the Covid lockdown period. The tenant pays half-rent for 12 months, relying on this promise. The landlord later demands the full arrears. Advise the tenant.
Consideration.answer:
The tenant should raise promissory estoppel (Central London Property Trust v High Trees [1947]). The elements are met:

1. existing legal relationship (lease)
2. clear, unambiguous promise by the landlord not to enforce strict rent obligations
3. the tenant relied on the promise by paying only half the rent.

It would be inequitable to allow the landlord to resile. The landlord is estopped from demanding arrears for the period of the promise. However: (a) promissory estoppel is a shield not a sword — the tenant cannot use it as a cause of action; (b) it may be suspensory only — on reasonable notice, the landlord could prospectively resume full rent; (c) the tenant cannot use it to escape the full debt permanently unless restitutio in integrum is impossible. The past arrears are likely extinguished; future full rent may resume on notice.
Consideration.summary_line: PROMISSORY ESTOPPEL · HIGH TREES · EXISTING RELATIONSHIP · RELIANCE · SUSPENSORY · SHIELD NOT SWORD · ARREARS EXTINGUISHED

Consideration.front: Stilk v Myrick [1809]
Consideration.issue: Whether sailors' promise to work harder to bring home a ship after desertion of two crew members was good consideration for the captain's promise of extra pay.
Consideration.rule: Performance of an existing contractual duty owed to the promisor is not good consideration for a fresh promise by the promisor. A party cannot demand extra payment for doing what they are already legally obliged to do.
Consideration.application: Two sailors deserted at Cronstadt. The captain promised the remaining crew extra pay if they worked the ship home. On return, the shipowner refused to pay the extra amount. The court held the sailors had provided no consideration — they were already contractually bound to work the ship, including dealing with emergencies such as desertion.
Consideration.conclusion: Existing contractual duty to the promisor = no consideration. Promise of extra pay unenforceable. Now partially modified by Williams v Roffey Bros [1991] in practical benefit cases.
Consideration.summary_line: STILK v MYRICK · EXISTING DUTY = NO CONSIDERATION · CONTRACTUAL OBLIGATION · MODIFIED BY WILLIAMS v ROFFEY

Consideration.front: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls (Contractors) Ltd [1991]
Consideration.issue: Whether a promise to pay a sub-contractor extra money to complete work already contracted for was supported by consideration, where the promisor obtained a practical benefit.
Consideration.rule: Where a promisor obtains a practical benefit from a promise to pay more for performance of an existing contractual duty, and the promise was not extracted by economic duress, that practical benefit can constitute good consideration.
Consideration.application: Roffey (main contractor) had engaged Williams (sub-contractor) to carry out carpentry. Williams ran into financial difficulty. Roffey, facing a penalty clause if the project was late, promised extra payments to ensure completion. Williams completed some flats but Roffey refused to pay. The Court of Appeal held: Roffey gained practical benefit (avoiding the penalty clause); that benefit was good consideration for the promise of extra payment.
Consideration.conclusion: Practical benefit = good consideration. Partially modifies Stilk v Myrick. Cannot be extended to part-payment of debts (Re Selectmove [1995]).
Consideration.summary_line: WILLIAMS v ROFFEY · PRACTICAL BENEFIT · NEW CONSIDERATION · EXISTING DUTY · NO ECONOMIC DURESS · NOT EXTENDED TO DEBT

Consideration.front: Foakes v Beer [1884]
Consideration.issue: Whether a creditor's agreement to accept part-payment in satisfaction of a judgment debt, with no further interest, was binding without fresh consideration.
Consideration.rule: Payment of a lesser sum of money cannot in law satisfy an obligation to pay a greater sum (the rule in Pinnel's Case [1602]). A creditor's agreement to accept less is not binding without consideration.
Consideration.application: Dr Foakes owed Mrs Beer a judgment debt of £2,090. She agreed he could pay in instalments with no further interest. He paid the full principal. She then claimed interest. The House of Lords held: his promise to pay what he already owed was not consideration for her promise to forgo interest. Pinnel's Case confirmed at the highest level.
Consideration.conclusion: Creditor not bound. Lesser sum rule (Pinnel's Case) confirmed by House of Lords. Promissory estoppel (High Trees) may provide equitable relief in appropriate cases.
Consideration.summary_line: FOAKES v BEER · LESSER SUM · PINNEL'S CASE CONFIRMED · HL · NO CONSIDERATION · ESTOPPEL MAY ASSIST

Consideration.front: Central London Property Trust Ltd v High Trees House Ltd [1947]
Consideration.issue: Whether a landlord who had promised to accept half rent during wartime was bound by that promise and could be estopped from claiming the full arrears.
Consideration.rule: Promissory estoppel: a party who makes a clear and unambiguous promise not to enforce their strict legal rights, on which the other party relies, may be estopped from resiling from that promise where it would be inequitable to do so.
Consideration.application: In 1940, the landlord agreed to halve the rent of a block of flats due to wartime conditions (most flats empty). By 1945, flats were fully occupied. Denning J held the landlord was estopped from claiming full rent during the wartime period when the promise had been relied upon. However, the landlord could resume full rent for 1945 onwards — the estoppel was suspensory, not extinctive.
Consideration.conclusion: Promissory estoppel established as a principle of English law. Past rent arrears during wartime extinguished by estoppel. Future full rent resumed on the changed circumstances. Shield, not sword (confirmed in Combe v Combe).
Consideration.summary_line: HIGH TREES · PROMISSORY ESTOPPEL · CLEAR PROMISE · RELIANCE · INEQUITABLE · SUSPENSORY · DENNING

Consideration.front: Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980]
Consideration.issue: Whether past acts could constitute valid consideration for a guarantee, and whether the agreement was voidable for economic duress.
Consideration.rule:
Past acts can constitute valid consideration where:

1. done at the promisor's request
2. both parties understood payment would follow
3. the subsequent promise merely fixes the amount.

Economic duress requires illegitimate pressure leaving no practical choice.
Consideration.application: Pao On owned shares in a company Lau wished to acquire. A complex arrangement required Pao On to retain shares. Pao On threatened to rescind unless given a guarantee of the share price. Lau provided a guarantee. The Privy Council held: the guarantee was supported by consideration via the past consideration exception. There was no economic duress — commercial pressure is insufficient; there must be illegitimate compulsion leaving no reasonable alternative.
Consideration.conclusion: Past consideration exception (Lampleigh v Brathwait) confirmed by PC. Economic duress requires more than hard bargaining — illegitimate pressure with no reasonable alternative.
Consideration.summary_line: PAO ON · PAST CONSIDERATION EXCEPTION · REQUEST + UNDERSTANDING + FIXES AMOUNT · ECONOMIC DURESS · ILLEGITIMATE PRESSURE

Consideration.front: Re McArdle [1951]
Consideration.issue: Whether work carried out on a house before a promise of payment could constitute valid consideration for that subsequent promise.
Consideration.rule: Past consideration is no consideration. An act that is wholly executed before a promise is made cannot constitute consideration for that promise — the promisee has given nothing in exchange for it.
Consideration.application: Mrs McArdle carried out improvements and repairs to a house that formed part of a family estate. After the work was completed, the adult children (who would inherit the estate) all signed a document promising to pay her £488 for the improvements. When the estate was wound up, the promise was not honoured. The Court of Appeal held that the work — having been completed before the promise — was past consideration and could not support the promise. Unenforceable.
Consideration.conclusion: Past consideration rule confirmed. No consideration: work was complete before the promise was made, and was not done at the promisors' request. The Lampleigh/Pao On exception did not apply.
Consideration.summary_line: RE McARDLE · PAST CONSIDERATION = NONE · WORK BEFORE PROMISE · UNENFORCEABLE · EXCEPTION NOT SATISFIED

Consideration.front: Combe v Combe [1951]
Consideration.issue: Whether a wife could use promissory estoppel as a cause of action to enforce her ex-husband's promise to pay her maintenance.
Consideration.rule: Promissory estoppel is a shield, not a sword. It can only be used defensively to prevent enforcement of strict legal rights — it cannot itself found an independent cause of action or create new contractual rights.
Consideration.application: After divorce, the husband promised to pay his wife £100 per year maintenance. She forbore from applying to the court for a maintenance order in reliance on his promise. He never paid. She sued on the promise, relying on promissory estoppel. The Court of Appeal, per Denning LJ, held: estoppel could not be used as a sword to create a new cause of action. There was also no consideration for the husband's promise.
Consideration.conclusion: Promissory estoppel cannot create new rights — only suspend or extinguish existing ones. It must be used defensively. Critical limit on the High Trees principle.
Consideration.summary_line: COMBE v COMBE · SHIELD NOT SWORD · NO CAUSE OF ACTION · DEFENSIVE ONLY · DENNING · NO CONSIDERATION

Consideration.front: Rosa helped her elderly neighbour move house last month. Grateful, the neighbour promised to pay Rosa £200 for her help. The neighbour has now refused to pay. Can Rosa enforce the promise?
Consideration.answer: No. Rosa's help was provided before the promise of £200 was made. This is past consideration — an act wholly executed before a promise is made cannot be consideration for that promise (Re McArdle [1951]; Roscorla v Thomas [1842]). The exception in Lampleigh v Brathwait (confirmed in Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980]) does not apply: Rosa did not act at the neighbour's request with a mutual understanding that payment would follow. She acted voluntarily. The neighbour's promise is therefore a gratuitous promise unsupported by consideration and is unenforceable. Rosa has no contractual remedy, though she may have a claim in unjust enrichment in certain circumstances.
Consideration.summary_line: PAST CONSIDERATION = NONE · RE McARDLE · NOT AT REQUEST · EXCEPTION FAILS · GRATUITOUS PROMISE · UNENFORCEABLE

Consideration.front: BuildRight Ltd is the main contractor on a development. It sub-contracts joinery work to CarpentryPlus for £60,000. Midway through the job, CarpentryPlus says it cannot continue unless it receives an additional £10,000. BuildRight agrees, fearing it will lose a significant bonus for on-time completion. CarpentryPlus completes the work. BuildRight now refuses to pay the extra £10,000. Advise CarpentryPlus.
Consideration.answer:
CarpentryPlus has a reasonable argument that the additional £10,000 is enforceable under Williams v Roffey Bros [1991]. The Court of Appeal held that where:

1. a party promises to pay more for performance of an existing contractual duty
2. the promisor obtains a practical benefit; and
3. the promise is not extracted by economic duress — the practical benefit can constitute good consideration.

Here, BuildRight obtained a practical benefit: avoiding the loss of its on-time bonus. However, CarpentryPlus should also address the duress point — BuildRight could argue the extra payment was extracted by economic duress (DSND Subsea v PGS [2000]). Whether there was genuine duress depends on whether CarpentryPlus's demand was illegitimate and left BuildRight with no reasonable alternative. On balance, if duress is not established, the £10,000 is likely enforceable.
Consideration.summary_line: WILLIAMS v ROFFEY · PRACTICAL BENEFIT · BONUS AVOIDED · EXISTING DUTY · ECONOMIC DURESS QUESTION · ENFORCEABLE

Consideration.front: Nico owes Elena £10,000 on a loan due 1 December. On 15 November, Nico offers to pay £7,000 now in full settlement. Elena agrees and accepts £7,000. On 2 December, Elena demands the remaining £3,000. Can she recover it?
Consideration.answer:
Yes, Elena can recover the £3,000 under the rule in Pinnel's Case [1602] (affirmed Foakes v Beer [1884]): payment of a lesser sum cannot in law satisfy a debt for a greater sum, even if the creditor agrees to accept it. Nico provided no additional consideration — he simply paid less than he owed. Elena's agreement was gratuitous and unenforceable for want of consideration. The recognised exceptions (earlier payment, payment in a different form, payment at a different place, part-payment by a third party) do not apply here — the debt was paid in the same form, at the same time (in fact, 15 days early). Wait — early payment is an exception to Pinnel's Case. If the payment of £7,000 was made earlier than the due date (1 December) at Elena's request, the earlier payment could constitute consideration and discharge the debt. On these facts, payment was made on 15 November — before the 1 December due date — which may satisfy the 'earlier payment' exception. Elena may therefore be unable to recover the £3,000.

⚠️ The early payment exception should be carefully considered on the specific facts.
Consideration.summary_line: PINNEL'S CASE · LESSER SUM · FOAKES v BEER · EARLY PAYMENT EXCEPTION · ⚠️ CHECK FACTS · ESTOPPEL ALTERNATIVE

Consideration.front: During the pandemic, a commercial landlord told its tenant restaurant: 'We will accept 50% of the rent for the next 12 months given your circumstances.' The tenant paid 50% for 12 months, relying on the promise. The landlord now claims all arrears. The tenant argues promissory estoppel. The landlord says it can do so because estoppel cannot extinguish contractual rights. Advise the tenant.
Consideration.answer: The tenant has a strong argument. The elements of promissory estoppel are met (High Trees [1947]): existing legal relationship (lease); clear unambiguous promise to accept reduced rent; reliance by the tenant (paid 50% for 12 months). It would be inequitable to allow the landlord to resile. The landlord's argument that estoppel cannot extinguish rights is partially correct — estoppel is classically suspensory (the landlord could in principle give reasonable notice to resume full rent prospectively). However, Denning J in High Trees suggested the arrears for the period of the promise were extinguished permanently. Where the tenant has so fundamentally altered their position in reliance that it would be impossible or unjust to require payment of arrears, the estoppel is likely extinctive for that period. Conclusion: past arrears for the 12-month period are likely extinguished. The landlord may give reasonable notice to resume full rent for future periods but cannot retrospectively claim arrears for the period of reliance.
Consideration.summary_line: PROMISSORY ESTOPPEL · HIGH TREES · RELIANCE · SUSPENSORY OR EXTINCTIVE · ARREARS LIKELY EXTINGUISHED · FUTURE RENT RESUMABLE

Consideration.front: Going deeper: Is the consideration doctrine coherent after Williams v Roffey Bros and Re Selectmove — and should it be reformed?
Consideration.answer: The doctrine of consideration requires that each party provide something of legal value in exchange for the other's promise. The rule in Stilk v Myrick [1809] — that performance of an existing contractual duty to the promisor cannot be consideration — was partially displaced by Williams v Roffey Bros [1991], which held that a 'practical benefit' to the promisor could suffice. Yet Re Selectmove [1995] refused to extend Williams v Roffey to debt reduction, preserving the rule in Pinnel's Case/Foakes v Beer [1884]. This creates a logical incoherence: if practical benefit is good consideration for a promise to pay more, why can it not support a promise to accept less? The Court of Appeal in Re Selectmove accepted it could not overrule a House of Lords decision (Foakes v Beer). Academic critique: Professor Atiyah argued consideration is a fiction — courts use it selectively to reach desired outcomes. The Law Commission (Working Paper No.60, 1975) recommended abolishing the requirement that consideration must move from the promisee, but no reform followed. Comparative note: most civil law systems and the UNIDROIT Principles do not require consideration — promissory liability arises from serious intent to be bound. The Austrian ABGB and French Code Civil manage without it. English law retains consideration partly for historical reasons and partly because promissory estoppel (High Trees) fills some of the gap. The doctrine's survival depends on whether certainty and formal bargain-marking justify its complexity.

Consideration.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Without being asked, Emma spends a weekend repairing her elderly neighbour's fence. The neighbour is delighted and says: 'That was wonderful — I will pay you £200 for doing that.' The neighbour later refuses to pay. Emma claims breach of contract.
Consideration.issue: The Trap: There is a clear promise (£200), a clearly performed act (repairing the fence), and a direct causal link between the two. Students identify offer + acceptance + performance and conclude there is a binding contract.
Consideration.rule: The Reality: The promise is unenforceable. Emma's act was past consideration — completed before any promise was made. Past consideration is no consideration (Re McArdle [1951]). The neighbour's promise is gratuitous and cannot be enforced in contract.
Consideration.application: Why the Distractor Fails: Consideration must be given in exchange for the promise — it must be the price of the promise. Emma acted entirely independently, without any request and without any expectation of payment. The temporal sequence is fatal: act completed → promise made. For valid consideration, the act and the promise must be part of the same bargain. The neighbour's promise is a gift promise, not a contractual one. The fact that the act was meritorious and the promise was clear does not create a contract without valid consideration.
Consideration.conclusion:
The Key Rule: Re McArdle — past consideration is no consideration. The Pao On v Lau Yiu Long [1980] exception only saves past acts where:

1. the act was done at the promisor's request
2. payment was understood by both parties to follow; and
3. such payment would have been enforceable if promised in advance.

Emma acted uninstructed, so none of these apply.
Consideration.summary_line: PAST CONSIDERATION · RE McARDLE · UNINSTRUCTED ACT · PAO ON EXCEPTION NOT MET · GRATUITOUS PROMISE

Consideration.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Hassan contracts to build an extension for £50,000. Halfway through, he tells the client the costs have risen and he needs an extra £10,000 to finish on time. The client agrees — she needs the work done before a planned sale of the property. On completion, the client refuses to pay the extra £10,000. Hassan claims it is owed.
Consideration.issue: The Trap: Hassan was already contractually bound to complete the extension for £50,000. Performing an existing contractual duty is not good consideration (Stilk v Myrick [1809]). Students apply Stilk and conclude the extra payment is unenforceable — Hassan provided no new consideration for the client's promise.
Consideration.rule: The Reality: The extra £10,000 may be enforceable under Williams v Roffey Bros [1991]. If the client obtained a practical benefit from the promise — here, securing timely completion before a planned property sale — that practical benefit can constitute consideration, provided the promise was not obtained by duress.
Consideration.application:
Why the Distractor Fails: Stilk v Myrick is not the end of the analysis after Williams v Roffey. The question is whether the client gained a practical benefit. Avoiding the delay and disruption of replacing Hassan mid-project and securing timely completion before a sale are recognised practical benefits. However, two questions must always be asked:

1. Was there a practical benefit? (Williams v Roffey consideration)
2. Was the promise freely given or extracted by an illegitimate threat? (economic duress).
