If Hassan threatened to abandon the job to extort the extra payment, the promise is voidable even if consideration technically exists.
Consideration.conclusion:
The Key Rule: Williams v Roffey — practical benefit to the promisor is valid consideration for a promise to pay more for existing performance. Stilk v Myrick is qualified but not overruled. Always check both: consideration (Williams v Roffey) AND duress (CON-040).

⚠️

**Note:** the Williams v Roffey exception does not apply to debt — promising to accept less in settlement of a debt still requires fresh consideration (Foakes v Beer).
Consideration.summary_line: EXISTING DUTY · STILK v MYRICK · WILLIAMS v ROFFEY · PRACTICAL BENEFIT · CHECK DURESS · FOAKES v BEER DEBT DISTINCTION

Intention to Create Legal Relations.front: What is the presumption regarding intention to create legal relations (ICTL) in commercial agreements?
Intention to Create Legal Relations.answer: In commercial agreements, there is a rebuttable presumption that parties intend to create legal relations (Edwards v Skyways [1964]). The burden lies on the party denying binding effect. Courts are slow to find no ICTL in commercial contexts. An 'honour clause' (e.g. 'binding in honour only') can rebut the presumption (Rose & Frank Co v Crompton Bros [1925]). Letters of comfort may or may not be binding depending on their wording.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.summary_line: COMMERCIAL = ICTL PRESUMED · EDWARDS v SKYWAYS · HONOUR CLAUSE REBUTS · ROSE & FRANK

Intention to Create Legal Relations.front: What is the presumption regarding ICTL in social and domestic agreements? When is it rebutted?
Intention to Create Legal Relations.answer:
In domestic and social agreements, there is a rebuttable presumption against ICTL (Balfour v Balfour [1919]). The presumption can be rebutted where:

1. parties are separated or dealing at arm's length (Merritt v Merritt [1970]: written agreement post-separation was binding)
2. significant financial stakes (Simpkins v Pays [1955]: pooled competition entries binding).

A formal, written agreement made after separation will generally be treated as binding.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.summary_line: DOMESTIC = NO ICTL · BALFOUR v BALFOUR · REBUTTED: SEPARATION · MERRITT · SIMPKINS v PAYS

Intention to Create Legal Relations.issue: Whether a husband's promise to pay his wife £30 per month maintenance while she remained in England was a legally binding contract.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.rule: In domestic and social arrangements between spouses or family members living in amity, there is a presumption that the parties do not intend to create legal relations. Such arrangements are not contracts.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.application: A husband working in Ceylon agreed to send his wife (who stayed in England for health reasons) £30 per month. The marriage later broke down and he stopped paying. The Court of Appeal held there was no contract — the agreement was a domestic arrangement, not intended to be legally binding. Atkin LJ: 'Agreements such as these are outside the realm of contracts altogether.'
Intention to Create Legal Relations.conclusion: No ICTL in domestic agreements made in amity. Presumption against legal relations. Rebutted on separation (Merritt v Merritt [1970]).
Intention to Create Legal Relations.summary_line: BALFOUR v BALFOUR · DOMESTIC PRESUMPTION · NO ICTL · SOCIAL AGREEMENT · AMITY · REBUTTED ON SEPARATION

Intention to Create Legal Relations.issue: Whether an agreement made between separated spouses regarding the matrimonial home was legally binding.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.rule: The Balfour v Balfour presumption against ICTL in domestic arrangements is rebutted where the parties are separated, dealing at arm's length, and especially where the agreement is reduced to writing.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.application: A husband and wife separated. The husband owned the matrimonial home. He signed a note agreeing that if his wife paid off the mortgage, he would transfer the house to her. She paid. He refused to transfer. The Court of Appeal held that the agreement was binding — the parties were no longer living in amity, were negotiating at arm's length, and the written document showed a serious intention.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.conclusion: Written agreement made after separation = legally binding contract. Balfour presumption displaced. Parties' arm's-length position is key.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.summary_line: MERRITT v MERRITT · SEPARATION · ARM'S LENGTH · WRITTEN AGREEMENT · ICTL REBUTTED · BINDING

Intention to Create Legal Relations.front: Marcus and his sister Diane agree that Diane will do Marcus's weekly grocery shopping in exchange for Marcus mowing her lawn every Sunday. After three weeks, Marcus stops mowing. Diane claims breach of contract. Advise Marcus.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.answer: Marcus has a strong defence. This is a domestic/social arrangement between family members — the presumption in Balfour v Balfour [1919] applies: parties to domestic and family arrangements are presumed not to intend legal relations. The burden falls on Diane to rebut this presumption. On these facts, there is no evidence of written agreement, no arm's-length dealing, and no indication that either party contemplated legal enforcement. The arrangement appears to be a casual domestic agreement, not a binding contract. Marcus is unlikely to be liable for breach. Diane would need to show clear evidence of intent to be legally bound to rebut the presumption.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.summary_line: DOMESTIC ARRANGEMENT · BALFOUR v BALFOUR · NO ICTL PRESUMED · BURDEN TO REBUT · NO WRITTEN AGREEMENT · NOT BINDING

Intention to Create Legal Relations.front: TechCorp and SupplyMax enter a commercial distribution agreement. The contract contains a clause reading: 'This arrangement is binding in honour only and shall not give rise to any legal obligations between the parties.' TechCorp later claims SupplyMax has breached the agreement. Can TechCorp enforce it?
Intention to Create Legal Relations.answer: No. In commercial agreements, there is a presumption that parties intend to create legal relations (Edwards v Skyways [1964]). However, this presumption is rebuttable — and an 'honour clause' expressly stating the arrangement is 'binding in honour only' is a recognised way of rebutting it (Rose & Frank Co v Crompton Bros [1925]: an 'honourable pledge clause' successfully prevented the commercial agreement from being legally enforceable). The clause here is unambiguous — both parties agreed the arrangement would not give rise to legal obligations. Courts give effect to such clauses. TechCorp cannot enforce the agreement as a legal contract. If TechCorp wished to have enforceable rights, it should not have agreed to include the honour clause.
Intention to Create Legal Relations.summary_line: COMMERCIAL = ICTL PRESUMED · HONOUR CLAUSE REBUTS · ROSE & FRANK · UNAMBIGUOUS · NOT ENFORCEABLE

Intention to Create Legal Relations.front: Going deeper: How strong is the commercial ICTL presumption, and what are its limits after Edwards v Skyways?
Intention to Create Legal Relations.answer:
Edwards v Skyways Ltd [1964] established that in commercial agreements, there is a strong presumption of an intention to create legal relations — and that the burden of rebutting it is heavy. Megaw J held that an 'ex gratia' payment promise in a commercial severance agreement was legally binding because the commercial context attracted the presumption, and nothing rebutted it. The presumption reflects the law's recognition that commercial parties expect legal enforceability. However, it is rebuttable: Rose & Frank Co v JR Crompton & Bros [1925] shows that an explicit 'honour clause' — 'binding in honour only' — can displace ICTL even in a commercial context. Limits and tensions:

1. Letters of intent and comfort letters present difficulty — are they binding? Kleinwort Benson v Malaysia Mining Corp [1989] held a letter of comfort ('it is our policy to ensure the company is always in a position to meet its liabilities') was not legally binding — it expressed present policy, not a binding promise.
2. Collective agreements between employers and trade unions are presumed not to be legally binding under TULRCA 1992 s.179 unless in writing and stating the contrary.
3. Heads of terms / MOU: typically stated to be 'subject to contract' and therefore non-binding.

The academic question is whether ICTL is a genuinely distinct requirement or merely a surrogate for other doctrinal requirements (consideration, certainty). Treitel treats it as a genuine requirement; some American scholars argue it adds little beyond consideration analysis.

Certainty and Completeness.front: What is the effect of uncertainty or incompleteness on a contract? How do courts approach this?
Certainty and Completeness.answer: A contract that is too uncertain or incomplete may be void (Scammell v Ouston [1941]: 'hire purchase terms' too vague). Courts try to uphold agreements where possible, using: implied terms; previous course of dealing; trade custom; severance of meaningless words. An 'agreement to agree' (terms to be fixed later) is not enforceable (May & Butcher v R [1934]). But where the framework is clear and only a minor gap remains, courts may fill it.
Certainty and Completeness.summary_line: UNCERTAINTY = VOID · SCAMMELL · COURTS UPHOLD · AGREEMENT TO AGREE UNENFORCEABLE · MAY & BUTCHER

Capacity.front: What are the three categories of contract a minor may enter into, and what is their legal effect?
Capacity.answer:
A minor is a person under 18 (Family Law Reform Act 1969). Three categories:

1. Binding contracts — contracts for necessaries (s.3 Sale of Goods Act 1979; Nash v Inman [1908]) and beneficial contracts of service (e.g. apprenticeships, education: Doyle v White City Stadium [1935]).
2. Voidable contracts — contracts giving an interest in a subject matter of a permanent or continuous nature (e.g. shares, partnership, land leases): binding unless the minor repudiates during minority or within a reasonable time of turning 18.
3. Void contracts — loans and contracts for non-necessaries are not enforceable against the minor; the minor is not bound but may enforce against the adult.
Capacity.summary_line: MINOR = UNDER 18 · BINDING: NECESSARIES + SERVICE · VOIDABLE: LAND/SHARES · VOID: LOANS · MINOR PROTECTED

Capacity.front: What are 'necessaries' for the purposes of a minor's contract and how did Nash v Inman [1908] apply the rule?
Capacity.answer: Necessaries are goods or services suitable to the condition in life of the minor and to their actual requirements at the time of sale and delivery (s.3 Sale of Goods Act 1979). In Nash v Inman [1908], a tailor supplied 11 fancy waistcoats to a Cambridge undergraduate. The Court of Appeal held the goods were not necessaries because the minor was already adequately supplied with clothing. The minor was therefore not bound. The supplier must prove both that the goods are capable of being necessaries AND that the minor was not already adequately supplied.
Capacity.summary_line: NECESSARIES · s3 SGA 1979 · SUITABLE TO CONDITION · NASH v INMAN · ADEQUATELY SUPPLIED

Capacity.front: How does mental incapacity or intoxication affect the validity of a contract?
Capacity.answer:
Mental incapacity — two-stage test: a contract made by a person lacking mental capacity is voidable (not void) at the incapacitated person's option if:

1. the person was unable to understand the nature of the contract at the time of making it; AND
2. the other party knew, or ought reasonably to have known, of the incapacity (Imperial Loan Co v Stone [1892]; Hart v O'Connor [1985] PC).

The 'ought to have known' limb is critical: the other party does not need actual knowledge — constructive knowledge suffices. If the incapacity was obvious from the circumstances, the contract is voidable even if the other party claims ignorance. If the other party had no knowledge and could not reasonably have known, the contract is binding. Under the Mental Capacity Act 2005 (s.2), a person lacks capacity if unable to understand, retain, use, or communicate a decision. Contracts for necessaries are enforceable at a reasonable price (s.7 MCA 2005). Intoxication: same two-stage test — voidable if the other party knew or ought to have known of the intoxication (Gore v Gibson [1843]). Must be disaffirmed promptly on regaining capacity.

⚠️ Exam trap: 'knew' alone is not the full test — 'ought to have known' is equally sufficient and frequently tested.
Capacity.summary_line: MENTAL INCAPACITY · TWO-STAGE TEST · UNABLE TO UNDERSTAND · KNEW OR OUGHT TO KNOW · IMPERIAL LOAN · HART v O'CONNOR · MCA 2005 · VOIDABLE

Capacity.front: Apply the necessaries test to each scenario involving a minor: (a) A 16-year-old purchases a £900 designer jacket. Her wardrobe already contains 12 winter coats. (b) A 17-year-old medical student purchases a set of medical textbooks needed for her course. (c) A 15-year-old purchases a luxury sports watch costing £4,000.
Capacity.answer:
The two-stage test (s.3 SGA 1979; Nash v Inman [1908]):

1. are the goods capable of being necessaries (suitable to the minor's condition in life)?
2. was the minor already adequately supplied? Both stages must be satisfied for the contract to bind. (a) Stage 1 — a jacket may be capable of being a necessary.

Stage 2 — fails: minor already has 12 coats and is adequately supplied. Not a necessary; minor not bound. (b) Stage 1 — medical textbooks are clearly suitable to a medical student's condition in life. Stage 2 — if the student did not already have equivalent books, she is not adequately supplied. Likely a necessary; minor bound to pay a reasonable price (not necessarily the contract price). (c) Stage 1 — a luxury sports watch is unlikely to be capable of being a necessary at all for a 15-year-old. Stage 2 need not be reached. Not a necessary; minor not bound.
Capacity.summary_line: NECESSARIES TWO-STAGE · CAPABLE + NOT ADEQUATELY SUPPLIED · NASH v INMAN · s3 SGA · PAY REASONABLE PRICE

Capacity.issue: Whether 11 fancy waistcoats supplied to a Cambridge undergraduate who was a minor were 'necessaries' binding him to pay.
Capacity.rule:
For goods to be necessaries binding on a minor, two conditions must both be satisfied:

1. the goods must be suitable to the minor's condition in life; AND
2. the minor must not already be adequately supplied with such goods at the time of sale and delivery (s.3 Sale of Goods Act 1979).
Capacity.application: A tailor supplied 11 fancy waistcoats to a Cambridge undergraduate who was a minor. The minor's father gave evidence that his son was already adequately provided with clothing. The Court of Appeal held: even if the waistcoats were capable of being necessaries (suitable to a Cambridge student's condition in life), the minor was already adequately supplied. The second limb was not satisfied. The tailor could not recover.
Capacity.conclusion: Two-stage test established. Both limbs must be proved by the supplier. Minor not bound. Supplier's failure to prove adequate supply is fatal to the claim.
Capacity.summary_line: NASH v INMAN · TWO-STAGE TEST · SUITABLE TO CONDITION · NOT ADEQUATELY SUPPLIED · MINOR NOT BOUND · s3 SGA

Capacity.front: Jake, aged 16, purchases a second-hand moped from a dealer for £1,500. Three months later the moped breaks down and Jake attempts to return it and recover his £1,500. The dealer refuses, arguing Jake agreed to purchase it 'as seen'. Advise Jake.
Capacity.answer: Jake is a minor (under 18). A moped may or may not be a necessary — it depends on his circumstances (s.3 SGA 1979; Nash v Inman [1908]). If it is a necessary (e.g. needed for travel to work or school) and Jake was not already adequately supplied, the contract binds him and he must pay a reasonable price. If it is not a necessary, the contract is not binding on Jake as a minor. In either case, the 'as seen' exclusion clause cannot exclude the implied terms under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 in a B2C contract (s.31 CRA 2015). The goods must be of satisfactory quality and fit for purpose. If the moped was not of satisfactory quality (breaking down after 3 months suggests it may not be), Jake has rights regardless of capacity — the CRA 2015 remedies apply (short-term right to reject, repair/replacement, price reduction). Jake should assert his CRA 2015 rights first; his minority adds an additional layer of protection.
Capacity.summary_line: MINOR · NECESSARIES TEST · NASH v INMAN · CRA 2015 · SATISFACTORY QUALITY · AS SEEN CLAUSE VOID · s31 CRA

Capacity.front: Patricia, who suffers from severe bipolar disorder and is in a manic episode, agrees to buy a luxury sports car for £85,000. She signs the finance agreement. When her episode passes, she seeks to rescind the contract. The dealer argues she appeared perfectly lucid at the time. Advise Patricia.
Capacity.answer:
Patricia may be able to rescind. Under the rule in Imperial Loan Co v Stone [1892] (and the Mental Capacity Act 2005), a contract made by a person lacking mental capacity is voidable if the other party knew of the incapacity at the time of contracting. The key question is whether the dealer knew or ought to have known of her incapacity. If the dealer had no knowledge and Patricia appeared lucid, the contract will be binding — a lack of mental capacity unknown to the other party does not make the contract voidable (to protect innocent third parties). Patricia bears the burden of proving:

1. she lacked capacity (under MCA 2005 s.2: could not understand, retain, use or weigh relevant information, or communicate a decision); and
2. the dealer knew of her incapacity.

If she can prove both elements, the contract is voidable at her option. She must act promptly — the car is not a necessary (s.7 MCA 2005 applies to necessaries only).
Capacity.summary_line: MENTAL INCAPACITY · VOIDABLE IF OTHER PARTY KNEW · MCA 2005 · IMPERIAL LOAN v STONE · BURDEN ON PATRICIA · NOT NECESSARY

Capacity.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

David suffers from bipolar disorder and signs a contract to buy an expensive sports car during a manic episode. He had no outward signs of distress; the dealer had no reason to suspect anything was wrong. David later seeks to have the contract set aside on the ground of mental incapacity.
Capacity.issue: The Trap: David lacked mental capacity when he signed — during a manic episode he could not understand the nature of the transaction. The obvious conclusion is that the contract is voidable: incapacity → voidable contract → David can rescind.
Capacity.rule: The Reality: The contract is not voidable on these facts. A contract with a person lacking mental capacity is only voidable if the other party knew or ought to have known of the incapacity (Imperial Loan Co v Stone [1892]; Hart v O'Connor [1985] PC). The dealer had no actual or constructive knowledge — the second limb of the test is not satisfied.
Capacity.application:
Why the Distractor Fails: Mental incapacity alone is not enough. The two-stage test is:

1. the person was unable to understand the nature of the contract; AND
2. the other party knew or ought reasonably to have known of the incapacity. Both limbs must be satisfied. David meets limb
1. but not limb (2).

The dealer saw no outward signs and had no reason to suspect. Allowing incapacity to void a contract regardless of the other party's knowledge would create serious commercial uncertainty — any party could escape a contract by later claiming incapacity.
Capacity.conclusion:
The Key Rule: Imperial Loan Co v Stone; Hart v O'Connor — mental incapacity makes a contract voidable only if the other party knew or ought to have known of the incapacity.

⚠️ 'Ought to have known' (constructive knowledge) is equally sufficient — but here the dealer could not reasonably have known. Incapacity without counterparty knowledge (actual or constructive) leaves the contract binding.
Capacity.summary_line: MENTAL INCAPACITY · TWO-STAGE TEST · IMPERIAL LOAN · HART v O'CONNOR · KNEW OR OUGHT TO KNOW · BOTH LIMBS REQUIRED · DEALER NO KNOWLEDGE

Express Terms and Representations.front: What is the parol evidence rule and what are its main exceptions?
Express Terms and Representations.answer:
The parol evidence rule: where parties have reduced their agreement to writing, extrinsic evidence (oral statements, prior negotiations) cannot add, vary, or contradict the written terms (Henderson v Arthur [1907]).

**Exceptions:**

1. collateral contracts
2. custom and trade usage
3. the written document is not the whole agreement
4. the document is void/voidable (misrepresentation)
5. rectification is sought.

The rule is weaker in modern contract law.
Express Terms and Representations.summary_line: PAROL EVIDENCE RULE · NO EXTRINSIC EVIDENCE · EXCEPTIONS: COLLATERAL CONTRACT · CUSTOM · RECTIFICATION

Express Terms and Representations.front: What factors do courts use to distinguish between a contractual term and a mere representation?
Express Terms and Representations.answer:
Factors suggesting a term:

1. the statement was of vital importance (Bannerman v White [1861]: buyer said he would not buy if hops were treated with sulphur — seller knew this was essential)
2. the maker had special skill or knowledge (Dick Bentley Productions v Harold Smith Motors [1965])
3. the other party was advised not to bother verifying it (Schawel v Reade [1913]).

Factor suggesting representation: significant time elapsed between statement and contract (Routledge v McKay [1954]).
Express Terms and Representations.summary_line: TERM vs REPRESENTATION · IMPORTANCE · SPECIAL KNOWLEDGE · DICK BENTLEY · VERIFICATION · ROUTLEDGE

Implied Terms.front: What are the two main tests for implying a term in fact into a contract?
Implied Terms.answer:
⚠️

1. Business efficacy test (The Moorcock [1889]): the term must be necessary to give the contract business efficacy — not merely reasonable or desirable.
2. Officious bystander test (Shirlaw v Southern Foundries [1939]): a term will be implied if it goes without saying — if an officious bystander suggested it, both parties would say 'of course!'.

The BP Refinery (Westernport) conditions [1977] (PC, Australia — widely applied but not strictly binding in English courts) set out five cumulative requirements: reasonable and equitable; necessary for business efficacy; obvious; capable of clear expression; consistent with express terms. In Marks & Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services [2015] (SC), the Supreme Court confirmed that 'necessity' remains the key requirement — a term will not be implied merely because it is reasonable.

⚠️ Recommend verifying against current SRA syllabus.
Implied Terms.summary_line: IMPLIED IN FACT · BUSINESS EFFICACY · THE MOORCOCK · OFFICIOUS BYSTANDER · SHIRLAW · BP REFINERY (PC) · M&S v BNP PARIBAS ⚠️

Implied Terms.front: What terms are implied by law into contracts for the sale of goods under the Sale of Goods Act 1979 (B2B)?
Implied Terms.answer:
Key implied conditions under SGA 1979: s.12 — seller has title to sell; s.13 — goods correspond with description; s.14(2) — goods are of satisfactory quality (where seller sells in the course of business); s.14(3) — goods are fit for a particular purpose made known to the seller; s.15 — goods sold by sample correspond with sample. All are conditions in B2B contracts.

**Note:** Consumer Rights Act 2015 applies to B2C contracts instead.
Implied Terms.summary_line: SGA 1979 · s12 TITLE · s13 DESCRIPTION · s14(2) QUALITY · s14(3) FITNESS · s15 SAMPLE · CONDITIONS

Implied Terms.front: What terms does the Consumer Rights Act 2015 imply into contracts for the sale of goods to consumers and what remedies are available?
Implied Terms.answer:
CRA 2015 implies: s.9 — satisfactory quality; s.10 — fit for purpose; s.11 — match description; s.13/14 — match sample or model. These cannot be excluded in consumer contracts (s.31). Remedies (hierarchy):

1. short-term right to reject (30 days)
2. repair or replacement
3. price reduction or final right to reject.

The CRA 2015 applies to B2C contracts only.
Implied Terms.summary_line: CRA 2015 · QUALITY · FITNESS · DESCRIPTION · 30-DAY REJECT · REPAIR · PRICE REDUCTION · NOT EXCLUDABLE

Implied Terms.front: Going deeper: What is the correct test for implying terms in fact — and did Marks & Spencer v BNP Paribas change the law?
Implied Terms.answer: The law of implied terms in fact has long centred on two tests: the business efficacy test (The Moorcock [1889]: a term is implied if necessary to give business efficacy to the contract) and the officious bystander test (Shirlaw v Southern Foundries [1939]: both parties would obviously assent to the term). The Privy Council in BP Refinery (Westernport) Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings [1977] proposed five cumulative conditions. In Marks & Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services [2015], the Supreme Court (Lord Neuberger) revisited the test and held: the correct touchstone is necessity, not reasonableness. A term should not be implied merely because it would be reasonable or even obvious — the court must be satisfied it is necessary to give the contract business efficacy, or that it goes without saying. The SC confirmed that 'obvious' and 'necessary' are overlapping but distinct concepts. Academic reaction: some commentators (including McMeel) argued Marks & Spencer raised the bar compared to earlier cases; others suggest the decision merely clarified what was always the law. The tension: courts routinely imply terms in complex commercial contracts without calling them 'necessary' — the label may not always reflect the reality of judicial gap-filling. The distinction between implying terms in fact and implying terms by law (e.g. SGA 1979, CRA 2015) is important: the latter operates automatically without the necessity test.

Classification of Terms.front: What is the difference between a condition, a warranty, and an innominate term in contract law?
Classification of Terms.answer: Condition: a term going to the root of the contract — breach entitles the innocent party to terminate AND claim damages, regardless of actual seriousness of breach (Poussard v Spiers [1876]). Warranty: a less important term — breach gives damages only (Bettini v Gye [1876]). Innominate term: remedy depends on consequences of the breach — if the breach deprives the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit, termination is available; otherwise damages only (Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962]).
Classification of Terms.summary_line: CONDITION = TERMINATE + DAMAGES · WARRANTY = DAMAGES ONLY · INNOMINATE = CONSEQUENCES · HK FIR

Classification of Terms.front: What test did the Court of Appeal apply in Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962] and what was its significance?
Classification of Terms.answer: The court held that some terms are innominate (intermediate) — neither pure condition nor warranty. The remedy depends on the consequences of the breach: if the breach deprives the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit, termination is available. If not, damages only. Significance: introduced flexibility into term classification, moving away from rigid condition/warranty dichotomy. However, it creates uncertainty — commercial parties often prefer terms to be classified as conditions for certainty. The seaworthiness clause was held innominate; the breach was not sufficiently serious to justify termination.
Classification of Terms.summary_line: HONG KONG FIR · INNOMINATE · SUBSTANTIALLY WHOLE BENEFIT · FLEXIBILITY vs CERTAINTY

Classification of Terms.front: A contract for the supply of a cargo vessel requires the ship to 'sail on 1 June'. The ship sails on 3 June and the cargo arrives only 2 days late. The buyer seeks to terminate. Advise whether this is possible, applying both the condition and innominate term approaches.
Classification of Terms.answer: Condition approach (The Mihalis Angelos [1971] supports time stipulations in commercial shipping being conditions): if 'sail on 1 June' is classified as a condition, any breach — however minor — entitles the buyer to terminate and claim damages. The 2-day delay is irrelevant; the right to terminate arises from breach of a condition, not from the seriousness of consequences. Innominate term approach (Hong Kong Fir [1962]): if the term is innominate, the court asks whether the breach deprived the buyer of substantially the whole benefit of the contract. A 2-day delay resulting in only 2 days' late arrival almost certainly does NOT satisfy this threshold — the buyer may only claim damages. Analysis: in a commercial shipping context, time clauses are frequently classified as conditions (certainty), which would allow termination. Courts must determine the proper classification from the contract and its commercial context.
Classification of Terms.summary_line: CONDITION = ANY BREACH → TERMINATE · INNOMINATE = CONSEQUENCES TEST · HK FIR · MIHALIS ANGELOS · TIME IN SHIPPING

Classification of Terms.front: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd [1962]
Classification of Terms.issue: Whether a breach of a seaworthiness clause in a charterparty entitled the charterer to terminate the contract.
Classification of Terms.rule: Some contractual terms are innominate (intermediate) — neither condition nor warranty. The right to terminate for breach depends not on the classification of the term, but on the nature and consequences of the breach: the innocent party may terminate only if the breach deprives them of substantially the whole benefit of the contract.
Classification of Terms.application: A ship was chartered for 24 months. It was found to be unseaworthy due to an incompetent engine room staff. Repairs took 20 weeks. The charterers purported to terminate. The Court of Appeal held: the seaworthiness clause was innominate. The 20-week delay did not deprive the charterers of substantially the whole benefit of a 24-month charter. Termination was unjustified; only damages were available.
Classification of Terms.conclusion: Innominate term category established. Remedy depends on the seriousness of consequences, not merely on classification. Introduced flexibility but creates uncertainty — commercial parties often prefer conditions.
Classification of Terms.summary_line: HONG KONG FIR · INNOMINATE TERM · SUBSTANTIALLY WHOLE BENEFIT · CONSEQUENCES TEST · FLEXIBILITY vs CERTAINTY

Classification of Terms.front: FashionHouse contracts with a supplier for 500 dresses to be delivered by 1 December for the Christmas season. The contract states: 'Time of delivery is of the essence.' The dresses arrive on 10 December. FashionHouse refuses the goods and treats the contract as terminated. The supplier argues the delay caused minimal loss. Is FashionHouse entitled to terminate?
Classification of Terms.answer: Yes. A term expressed to be 'of the essence' is a condition — any breach, however minor in its consequences, entitles the innocent party to terminate and claim damages (Poussard v Spiers [1876]). The consequences of the breach are irrelevant to the right to terminate where a condition has been breached. The supplier's argument that the delay caused minimal loss has no bearing on FashionHouse's right to terminate — that argument would be relevant only under the innominate term approach (Hong Kong Fir [1962]), which applies where the term has not been expressly classified as a condition. Here, the express 'time of the essence' clause classifies delivery time as a condition. FashionHouse is entitled to reject the goods, terminate the contract, and claim damages for any losses flowing from the breach.
Classification of Terms.summary_line: TIME OF ESSENCE = CONDITION · ANY BREACH = TERMINATE · CONSEQUENCES IRRELEVANT · POUSSARD · CONTRAST INNOMINATE TERM

Classification of Terms.front: A contract for the hire of a concert venue for 50 nights states that the venue will be 'adequately lit and heated'. On one night, a heating unit fails and the temperature drops slightly below comfortable. The promoter purports to terminate the entire contract. Advise the venue.
Classification of Terms.answer: The venue should resist termination. 'Adequately lit and heated' is an innominate term — it is not a pure condition (not going to the root of the contract) nor a warranty (it is capable of serious breach). The remedy depends on the consequences of the breach (Hong Kong Fir Shipping [1962]). The test is whether the breach deprives the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract. Here, one night's slightly inadequate heating out of 50 nights does not come close to depriving the promoter of substantially the whole benefit. Termination is not available. The promoter's remedy is damages only — limited to losses flowing from the one defective night. Attempting to terminate the whole 50-night contract would itself be a repudiatory breach by the promoter.
Classification of Terms.summary_line: INNOMINATE TERM · HONG KONG FIR · SUBSTANTIALLY WHOLE BENEFIT · ONE NIGHT ≠ TERMINATION · DAMAGES ONLY · PROMOTER REPUDIATES

Classification of Terms.front: A contract for the sale of a car describes it as 'a 2019 model'. The car is actually a 2017 model. The buyer, Daniel, wants to terminate. Separately, the seller warranted the car has 'a full service history' but one service stamp is missing. Daniel wants to claim for this too. Advise Daniel on his remedies for each.
Classification of Terms.answer: First issue — year of manufacture: s.13 SGA 1979 (B2B) or s.11 CRA 2015 (B2C) implies a condition that goods correspond with their description. Selling a 2017 car as a 2019 model is a breach of the description condition. This is a condition — Daniel is entitled to reject the goods, terminate the contract, and recover the purchase price, regardless of whether he suffered significant loss. Second issue — service history warranty: a warranty is a less important term — breach gives rise to a claim in damages only, not termination (s.11(3) SGA; Bettini v Gye [1876]). The missing service stamp may reduce the car's value — Daniel can recover damages for that diminution. He cannot use the warranty breach as a ground for termination.
Classification of Terms.summary_line: DESCRIPTION = CONDITION · s13 SGA · TERMINATE + REJECT · WARRANTY = DAMAGES ONLY · BETTINI v GYE · TWO SEPARATE REMEDIES

Classification of Terms.front: Going deeper: Does The Hansa Nord [1976] show that courts systematically prefer to classify terms as innominate — and what are the implications for commercial certainty?
Classification of Terms.answer: The Hansa Nord (Cehave NV v Bremer Handelsgesellschaft) [1976] involved a contract for the sale of citrus pulp pellets. The goods were partly damaged on arrival. The buyer rejected the entire cargo, relying on the condition that goods be shipped in 'good condition'. The market had fallen and the buyer bought the same cargo back cheaply at auction, using it for its original purpose. The Court of Appeal (Roskill LJ) held: the 'good condition' term was not a condition — it was an innominate term. Because the breach did not deprive the buyer of substantially the whole benefit of the contract, termination was not available. The buyer was entitled only to damages. Significance: The Hansa Nord extended the Hong Kong Fir [1962] approach beyond employment contracts into sale of goods — a significant departure, since SGA 1979 terms (ss.13–15) had traditionally been treated as conditions. The court prioritised commercial substance over technical classification, preventing a buyer from exploiting a minor breach to escape a bad bargain in a falling market. Academic tension: Treitel and Reynolds argued that the innominate term approach reduces certainty — commercial parties need to know in advance whether a breach allows termination. Conditions provide that certainty; innominate terms do not. The Hong Kong Fir / Hansa Nord approach requires post-hoc assessment of the consequences of breach, which is unpredictable at the time of contracting. The unresolved tension: English law now has both strict SGA conditions (e.g. s.13 — description) where minor breaches technically allow rejection, and innominate terms where they do not. The boundary between the two remains contested — particularly for s.15A SGA 1979 (non-consumer buyer cannot reject for slight breach of condition). The Law Commission recommended statutory reform in 1987; s.15A SGA was the partial response.

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What are the three common law routes for incorporating an exclusion clause into a contract?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
1. Signature: a party who signs a document containing the clause is bound even without reading it (L'Estrange v Graucob [1934]), subject to misrepresentation or non est factum.
2. Reasonable notice: the clause must be brought to the other party's attention before or at the time of contracting (Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971]); unusual/onerous clauses require greater notice (Interfoto Picture Library v Stiletto [1989]).
3. Course of dealing: consistent prior dealings may incorporate the clause (Spurling v Bradshaw [1956]).
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: EXCLUSION: SIGNATURE · NOTICE · COURSE OF DEALING · L'ESTRANGE · THORNTON · INTERFOTO

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What is the contra proferentem rule and how does it apply to negligence clauses?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: Contra proferentem: where an exclusion or limitation clause is ambiguous, it is construed against the party relying on it (the proferens). It is a rule of last resort, applied where genuine ambiguity remains after ordinary interpretation. For negligence clauses, courts require clear and express words before liability for negligence is excluded (Canada Steamship Lines v The King [1952]). The more onerous the clause, the greater the clarity required.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CONTRA PROFERENTEM · AMBIGUITY AGAINST PROFERENS · NEGLIGENCE: CLEAR WORDS · CANADA STEAMSHIP

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What does s.2 UCTA 1977 provide regarding exclusion of liability for negligence causing death, personal injury, and other loss?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
UCTA 1977 s.2 applies to business liability (i.e. where the breach of duty arises in the course of a business): s.2

1. — a person cannot by any contract term or notice exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence — absolutely void. s.2
2. — liability for other loss or damage caused by negligence may only be excluded if the term satisfies the reasonableness test (s.11 UCTA). UCTA applies to all business liability; in B2C contracts the Consumer Rights Act 2015 now takes precedence for contractual terms, but UCTA s.2
1. on death/personal injury applies in all contexts involving business liability.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: UCTA 1977 s2 · DEATH/INJURY: CANNOT EXCLUDE (ALL BUSINESS LIABILITY) · OTHER LOSS: REASONABLENESS TEST · CRA 2015 FOR B2C TERMS

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What is the reasonableness test under s.11 UCTA 1977 and who bears the burden of proof?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: A term must have been fair and reasonable to include having regard to the circumstances known to the parties when the contract was made (s.11(1)). Schedule 2 guidelines (relevant to ss.6–7 but used more widely): relative bargaining strength; whether an inducement was received; whether the customer knew or should have known of the term; whether it was practicable to comply with any condition; whether goods were custom-made. The burden of proving reasonableness lies on the party relying on the clause.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: UCTA REASONABLENESS · BARGAINING STRENGTH · INDUCEMENT · KNOWLEDGE · SCHEDULE 2 · BURDEN ON PROFERENS

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What protection does the Consumer Rights Act 2015 provide against unfair terms in consumer contracts?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: CRA 2015, Part 2: a term in a B2C contract is unfair if, contrary to good faith, it causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of the consumer (s.62). Unfair terms are not binding on the consumer (s.62(1)). Core terms (price and subject matter) are exempt from the fairness assessment if transparent and prominent (s.64). Schedule 2 contains a non-exhaustive 'grey list' of potentially unfair terms. The contract may continue without the unfair term if capable of surviving.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 · SIGNIFICANT IMBALANCE · GOOD FAITH · UNFAIR = NOT BINDING · CORE TERMS EXEMPT · GREY LIST

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: A customer parks her car in a car park and pays at the barrier. As she walks to her car she notices a sign on a pillar stating: 'The management accepts no liability for loss or damage to vehicles however caused.' Her car is damaged by a falling barrier. Is the exclusion clause incorporated?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: No — the exclusion clause is not incorporated. The contract was formed when the customer paid at the barrier (offer + acceptance + consideration). A term can only be incorporated by notice if it is brought to the other party's attention BEFORE or AT the time of contracting (Olley v Marlborough Court [1949]: a notice in a hotel bedroom could not incorporate a term because the contract was concluded at the reception desk). The clause on the pillar was displayed after the contract was formed and therefore cannot be incorporated by notice. Additionally, by analogy with Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971], very onerous terms excluding liability require particularly prominent notice before contracting. The exclusion is ineffective.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: INCORPORATION BY NOTICE · BEFORE OR AT TIME OF CONTRACT · OLLEY v MARLBOROUGH COURT · THORNTON · POST-CONTRACT = INEFFECTIVE

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, what is a consumer's short-term right to reject goods, and how long does it last?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: Under s.20 CRA 2015, a consumer has the right to reject goods that do not conform to contract (i.e. breach of s.9 satisfactory quality, s.10 fit for purpose, or s.11 as described) and receive a full refund. This right lasts for 30 days from the date the consumer took ownership of (or received, if later) the goods. If the consumer exercises this right within 30 days, the trader cannot insist on repair or replacement instead — the consumer is entitled to a full refund. The 30-day period may be reduced if the goods are perishable (s.22(3)). After 30 days but within 6 months: the consumer must first allow the trader one attempt at repair or replacement (s.23). If that fails, is impossible, or takes too long, the consumer can then claim a price reduction or final right to reject (s.24). After 6 months: the consumer must prove the defect existed at the time of purchase (s.24(9)).
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 s20 · SHORT-TERM RIGHT TO REJECT · 30 DAYS · FULL REFUND · B2C · PERISHABLES REDUCED

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What are a consumer's remedies under the CRA 2015 if faulty goods are returned after the 30-day short-term right to reject has expired but within 6 months of purchase?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
Once the 30-day short-term right to reject (s.20 CRA 2015) has expired, a consumer who discovers a fault within 6 months of delivery must first give the trader one opportunity to repair or replace the goods (s.23 CRA 2015).

**Key rules:**

1. The trader must carry out repair or replacement within a reasonable time and without significant inconvenience to the consumer (s.23(2)).
2. The consumer chooses between repair and replacement, unless the chosen option is disproportionate compared to the other (s.23(3)).
3. There is only one attempt — if repair/replacement fails, is unavailable, or would cause significant inconvenience, the consumer may move to the second tier: price reduction or final right to reject under s.24.
4. Within 6 months, the defect is presumed to have been present at time of delivery (s.24(9)) — the burden is on the trader to disprove this.

Price reduction is proportionate to the diminution in value; final rejection gives a refund subject to a deduction for use.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 s23 · REPAIR OR REPLACE FIRST · ONE ATTEMPT · s24 PRICE REDUCTION · FINAL REJECT · 6 MONTHS PRESUMPTION

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What are the implied terms and consumer remedies for digital content and services under the CRA 2015?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
Services (ss.49–57 CRA 2015): The trader must perform the service with reasonable care and skill (s.49); if a timeframe is agreed, perform within it (s.52); if a price is not agreed, charge only a reasonable price (s.51). Remedies for breach of s.49 (care and skill):

1. Repeat performance — the trader must redo the service to the required standard (s.55). This is the primary remedy.
2. Price reduction — if repeat performance is impossible or would take unreasonably long, the consumer may claim a price reduction of up to 100% (s.56).

Digital content (ss.34–47 CRA 2015): Must be of satisfactory quality (s.34), fit for purpose (s.35), and as described (s.36). Remedies: repair or replacement (s.43); price reduction or refund (s.44). Trader is also liable for damage caused to a consumer's device or other digital content by faulty digital content (s.46).
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 · SERVICES s49 · CARE AND SKILL · REPEAT PERFORMANCE s55 · PRICE REDUCTION s56 · DIGITAL CONTENT ss34-47

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Under UCTA 1977, which exclusion clauses are automatically void and which must satisfy the reasonableness test in a B2B context?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: Automatically void under UCTA 1977 (cannot be excluded regardless of context): s.2(1): liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence cannot be excluded or restricted by any contract term or notice — applies to all business liability. Must satisfy the reasonableness test (s.11 + Schedule 2) in B2B contracts: s.2(2): liability for other loss or damage caused by negligence (e.g. property damage, financial loss) — reasonable if the clause was fair and reasonable having regard to the circumstances known at contracting. s.6(2) (now s.6(1A) post-CRA): implied terms under SGA 1979 (ss.13–15, title excluded) in B2B — must satisfy reasonableness. s.3: where one party deals on the other's written standard terms, exclusion of breach liability or right to claim different/no performance must satisfy reasonableness. Schedule 2 reasonableness factors: relative bargaining strength; inducement received; whether party knew or ought to have known of the term; whether goods were custom-made; practical consequences of the decision (George Mitchell v Finney Lock Seeds [1983]).
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: UCTA 1977 · s2(1) VOID · DEATH/INJURY ABSOLUTE · s2(2) REASONABLENESS · s11 · SCHEDULE 2 · GEORGE MITCHELL

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What is the key distinction between UCTA 1977 and the CRA 2015 in regulating exclusion clauses, and which applies in a given contract?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: The two regimes operate in parallel but apply to different contracts: CRA 2015 applies to B2C contracts — where a trader deals with a consumer (an individual acting wholly or mainly outside their trade/business/profession: s.2 CRA). The CRA 2015 replaced UCTA 1977 and UTCCR 1999 for consumer contracts. Under CRA 2015: terms excluding trader liability for death/personal injury from negligence are always unfair (s.65); other terms are assessed for fairness under s.62 (significant imbalance, contrary to good faith); the transparency requirement applies (s.68). Core terms (price and main subject matter) are exempt from fairness assessment if prominent and transparent (s.64). UCTA 1977 applies to B2B contracts — where both parties act in the course of business. s.2(1) absolutely prohibits excluding death/personal injury liability. Other clauses must pass the reasonableness test (s.11). Key trap: UCTA 1977 also applies to business-to-consumer contracts for negligence (s.2) — but for contract terms in B2C contracts, CRA 2015 is the primary regime. In practice, always identify the parties' status first: consumer or business?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 = B2C · UCTA 1977 = B2B · FAIRNESS TEST · REASONABLENESS TEST · IDENTIFY PARTIES FIRST · OVERLAP ON NEGLIGENCE

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Under the CRA 2015, does the 30-day short-term right to reject period pause during repair or replacement — and why does this matter?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
Yes. Under s.22(6) CRA 2015, the 30-day short-term right to reject period is suspended (stops running) from the time the consumer requests or agrees to repair or replacement, and resumes when the goods are returned to the consumer in a repaired or replaced state. This prevents the trader from delaying repair until the 30-day window has expired, thereby defeating the consumer's right to a full refund. Example: consumer buys goods on day 1; fault appears on day 25; consumer requests repair on day 25; the 30-day clock stops. Repair takes 20 days; goods returned on day 45. The clock resumes — the consumer still has 5 days of the original 30-day period remaining to reject if the repair has not resolved the fault.

⚠️ Exam tip: if the repaired goods still do not conform, the consumer may reject within the remaining period and claim a full refund — or move to the s.24 price reduction / final right to reject route if the 30-day window has now fully expired. This suspension rule is a specific SRA-tested nuance of s.22 CRA 2015.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 s22(6) · 30-DAY CLOCK PAUSES · DURING REPAIR/REPLACE · RESUMES ON RETURN · PREVENTS TRADER DELAY

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What is the correct three-stage analytical framework for any exclusion or limitation clause question?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
Every exclusion clause question must be worked through in order — a clause fails if it falls at any stage: Stage 1 — Incorporation: is the clause part of the contract? Three routes: (a) signature (L'Estrange v Graucob [1934] — bound even if unread); (b) reasonable notice before or at contracting (Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971] — notice after contract formed is too late; onerous terms need special attention: Interfoto [1989]); (c) course of dealing (Spurling v Bradshaw [1956]). Stage 2 — Construction: does the clause cover the loss on its true interpretation? Contra proferentem — ambiguity construed against the party relying on the clause. Clear words required to exclude negligence liability (Canada Steamship [1952] guidelines). Stage 3 — Statutory control: even if incorporated and construed to cover the loss, does UCTA 1977 (B2B) or CRA 2015 (B2C) invalidate it? s.2

1. UCTA: death/personal injury from negligence — always void. s.2
2. UCTA / s.65 CRA: other negligence loss — reasonableness (B2B) or fairness (B2C).

⚠️ Exam method: always address all three stages in order. A clause may be incorporated and clearly worded but still void under statute.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: EXCLUSION CLAUSE FRAMEWORK · INCORPORATION · CONSTRUCTION · STATUTORY CONTROL · UCTA · CRA · THREE STAGES IN ORDER

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: What is the CRA 2015 consumer remedies sequence for faulty goods, and how do the three tiers interact?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
The CRA 2015 creates a structured three-tier remedies sequence for goods that do not conform to contract (breach of ss.9–11). Students must apply the tiers in order: Tier 1 — Short-term right to reject (s.20): within 30 days of delivery, the consumer may reject and claim a FULL REFUND. The trader cannot insist on repair or replacement instead. The 30-day clock is paused during any repair/replacement and resumes when the goods are returned (s.22(6)). Tier 2 — Repair or replacement (s.23): after 30 days (or if the consumer chose not to reject within 30 days), the consumer may require repair or replacement. The consumer chooses which — unless the chosen option is disproportionate. ONE attempt only. Within 6 months: defect presumed to have been present at delivery (s.24(9)) — trader bears the burden of proof. Tier 3 — Price reduction or final right to reject (s.24): if Tier 2 fails, is impossible, or would cause significant inconvenience, the consumer may claim: (a) price reduction (proportionate to diminution in value), or (b) final right to reject (refund, subject to deduction for use after first 30 days). After 6 months: consumer must prove the defect existed at time of delivery.

⚠️ Work through the tiers in sequence — identify which tier applies on the facts.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 · THREE TIERS · s20 REJECT 30 DAYS · s23 REPAIR/REPLACE · s24 PRICE REDUCTION · FINAL REJECT · CLOCK PAUSES · 6 MONTH PRESUMPTION

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Under the CRA 2015, how does the burden of proof shift depending on when a defect in goods is discovered?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
The CRA 2015 creates a time-based burden of proof rule for non-conforming goods: 0–30 days (short-term right to reject, s.20): the consumer may reject and claim a full refund. No burden of proof issue — the 30-day period is absolute. 0–6 months (s.24(9)): if the consumer claims repair, replacement, price reduction, or the final right to reject, the defect is PRESUMED to have been present at the time of delivery. The TRADER bears the burden of proving the goods did conform at delivery (i.e. that the defect arose after sale). This is a significant consumer protection — the consumer does not need to prove the cause of the defect. After 6 months (up to 6 years, Limitation Act 1980): the presumption reverses. The CONSUMER must prove that the defect existed at the time of delivery. This is harder — the consumer bears the burden of establishing a latent defect was present from the outset. The 6-year long-stop: contract claims must be brought within 6 years of the breach (Limitation Act 1980 s.5). After 6 years, the claim is time-barred.

⚠️ Exam summary: 0–6 months: trader proves defect was NOT there at delivery. 6 months–6 years: consumer proves defect WAS there at delivery. This burden flip is a frequent SQE distractor in consumer goods questions.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 s24(9) · 0-6 MONTHS TRADER PROVES · 6 MONTHS-6 YEARS CONSUMER PROVES · BURDEN FLIP · LIMITATION ACT 6 YEARS · PRESUMPTION REVERSED

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: L'Estrange v F Graucob Ltd [1934]
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.issue: Whether a party who signs a contractual document is bound by its terms even if she has not read them.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.rule: A party who signs a document containing contractual terms is bound by those terms, whether or not she has read them, in the absence of fraud, misrepresentation, or non est factum.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.application: Mrs L'Estrange bought a cigarette vending machine and signed a sales agreement containing small-print exclusion clauses excluding all conditions and warranties. The machine was defective. She claimed it was unfit for purpose. The court held she was bound by the exclusion clause — her signature incorporated it regardless of whether she had read or understood it.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.conclusion: Signature = incorporation of all terms in the signed document. No requirement to draw attention to specific terms where the document is signed. Contrasts with the notice cases (Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking).
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: L'ESTRANGE v GRAUCOB · SIGNATURE = INCORPORATION · NOT READ = STILL BOUND · FRAUD/MISREP EXCEPTION · CONTRAST NOTICE CASES

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking Ltd [1971]
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.issue: Whether an exclusion clause displayed on a ticket issued by an automated machine and on notices inside the car park was incorporated into the contract.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.rule: A clause can only be incorporated by notice if it is brought to the other party's attention before or at the time the contract is made. Post-contractual notices are ineffective. Particularly onerous terms require a higher degree of notice.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.application: Thornton drove into an automated car park, paid at a machine which issued a ticket. The ticket referred to conditions displayed on a notice inside the car park, including a personal injury exclusion. Thornton was injured. The Court of Appeal held: the contract was formed when Thornton drove up to the machine and it accepted his money. The conditions on the ticket and inside were post-contractual and not incorporated. The personal injury exclusion would in any event have required very prominent notice.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.conclusion: Exclusion clause not incorporated — terms must be communicated before contract is formed. Onerous terms require heightened notice. Foundational case for timing of notice.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: THORNTON · SHOE LANE PARKING · NOTICE BEFORE CONTRACT · POST-CONTRACT = INEFFECTIVE · ONEROUS TERMS · TIMING

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Interfoto Picture Library Ltd v Stiletto Visual Programmes Ltd [1989]
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.issue: Whether an unusually onerous clause in a delivery note was incorporated into the contract by the general reference to conditions on the note.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.rule: Where a contractual term is particularly unusual or onerous, the party relying on it must take reasonable steps to draw that specific term to the other party's attention — general notice of the existence of conditions is insufficient.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.application: Interfoto sent 47 photographic transparencies to Stiletto with a delivery note containing a clause charging £5 per transparency per day for late return — a very high charge. Stiletto returned them late without noticing the clause. The Court of Appeal held: the clause was so onerous and unusual that Interfoto was required to take specific steps to bring it to Stiletto's attention. General reference to conditions was insufficient. The clause was not incorporated.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.conclusion: Onerous clauses require specific and sufficient notice. General reference to conditions cannot incorporate an unusually burdensome term. The more onerous, the greater the notice required.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: INTERFOTO · ONEROUS CLAUSE · SPECIFIC NOTICE REQUIRED · GENERAL NOTICE INSUFFICIENT · UNUSUALLY BURDENSOME

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Photo Production Ltd v Securicor Transport Ltd [1980]
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.issue: Whether the doctrine of fundamental breach prevented an exclusion clause from operating where the breach was caused deliberately by the defendant's employee.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.rule: There is no rule of law that a fundamental breach of contract prevents an exclusion clause from operating. Exclusion clauses must be assessed by their natural meaning in context, and by relevant statutory controls (UCTA 1977) — not by a special judge-made rule.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.application: Securicor contracted to provide security for Photo Production's factory. A Securicor guard deliberately started a fire, destroying the factory (loss of approximately £615,000). Securicor relied on an exclusion clause limiting liability. The House of Lords abolished the doctrine of fundamental breach as a rule of law: the clause, properly construed, was wide enough to cover the breach. The parties were commercial equals who had freely allocated the risk by contract.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.conclusion: Doctrine of fundamental breach abolished. Exclusion clauses are subject to construction and UCTA 1977 — not a special overriding rule. Parties free to allocate risk by contract.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: PHOTO PRODUCTION · FUNDAMENTAL BREACH ABOLISHED · EXCLUSION CLAUSE VALID · CONSTRUCTION · UCTA 1977 · RISK ALLOCATION

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Gemma signs a gym membership contract without reading it. The contract contains a clause excluding the gym's liability for personal injury caused by faulty equipment. She is later injured when a treadmill malfunctions. She claims she did not know about the clause. Can she rely on her ignorance?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: Generally no — but the clause is likely void regardless. On incorporation: by signing the membership contract, Gemma is bound by all its terms whether or not she read them (L'Estrange v Graucob [1934]). Her ignorance of the clause does not prevent its incorporation — signature = incorporation. However, the clause itself is unenforceable under UCTA 1977 s.2(1): a person acting in the course of business cannot exclude or restrict liability for personal injury or death caused by negligence. This is an absolute prohibition — it cannot be overridden regardless of how clearly the clause is worded or how prominently it is displayed. The gym cannot rely on the exclusion clause and remains liable for Gemma's personal injury. She should pursue a claim in negligence.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: SIGNATURE = INCORPORATION · L'ESTRANGE v GRAUCOB · IGNORANCE NO DEFENCE · UCTA s2(1) · PERSONAL INJURY CANNOT EXCLUDE · VOID

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: FreightFast and MegaStore have traded together for 10 years. Every delivery contract during this time has been on FreightFast's standard terms, which include a clause limiting liability to £500 per consignment. MegaStore has never read the terms. A consignment worth £50,000 is lost. FreightFast relies on the £500 limit. Is the clause incorporated?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: Very likely yes. Even where a party has not read the terms, a clause can be incorporated through a consistent course of dealing (Spurling v Bradshaw [1956]; McCutcheon v David MacBrayne [1964]). Ten years of dealing on the same terms constitutes a sufficiently consistent and regular course of dealing. MegaStore should have known of the limitation clause. The clause is likely incorporated. However, MegaStore should also consider UCTA 1977: in a B2B contract, a limitation clause must satisfy the reasonableness test (s.11 and Schedule 2). Relevant factors: relative bargaining strength; whether MegaStore received an inducement (e.g. lower rates); whether MegaStore could have insured the goods. A £500 limit on a £50,000 consignment may fail the reasonableness test, but courts are slow to interfere with commercial contracts negotiated between businesses of equal bargaining power.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: COURSE OF DEALING · 10 YEARS · SPURLING v BRADSHAW · INCORPORATED · UCTA REASONABLENESS · B2B · SCHEDULE 2

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Oliver books a language course online. Buried in the 47-page terms and conditions is clause 31(b): 'In the event of course cancellation, the student forfeits 100% of all fees paid with no right to refund or substitution.' Oliver's course is cancelled. The provider relies on clause 31(b). Assess whether this clause is effective.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: The clause faces challenges on two fronts. First, incorporation: the clause is buried in 47 pages of terms. For particularly onerous or unusual clauses, the party relying on them must take reasonable steps to draw them specifically to the other party's attention (Interfoto Picture Library v Stiletto [1989]). A clause forfeiting 100% of fees on cancellation (without any fault by the student) is highly unusual and onerous — a general reference to 'terms and conditions' is likely insufficient. Second, fairness under CRA 2015: this is a B2C contract. A term that causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the consumer's detriment, contrary to good faith, is unfair and not binding (s.62 CRA 2015). Forfeiting 100% of fees on the provider's own cancellation is a strong candidate for the grey list (Schedule 2, para.1(d)). The clause is likely neither incorporated nor enforceable against Oliver.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: ONEROUS CLAUSE · INTERFOTO · SPECIFIC NOTICE REQUIRED · CRA 2015 · UNFAIR TERM · SIGNIFICANT IMBALANCE · GREY LIST

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Priya buys a new washing machine from an electrical retailer for £600 on 1 March. On 28 March — 27 days later — it floods her kitchen due to a manufacturing defect. The retailer offers to repair it. Priya wants a full refund. Who is right?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: Priya is right. Under s.20 CRA 2015, a consumer has a short-term right to reject goods that do not conform to contract within 30 days of delivery and receive a full refund. The washing machine is not of satisfactory quality (s.9 CRA 2015) — flooding a kitchen 27 days after purchase clearly evidences a defect present at the time of sale. Priya is still within the 30-day window. Critically, within the 30-day period the trader cannot insist on repair or replacement instead of a refund — the consumer's right to reject and receive a full refund is absolute during this period. The retailer's offer of repair is not what Priya is entitled to demand at this stage. Priya should reject the goods in clear terms, return the machine, and demand a full refund of £600. She may also have a claim for any consequential losses (damage to kitchen) under general contract principles, subject to the retailer's liability being established.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 s20 · 30 DAYS · 27 DAYS = WITHIN · FULL REFUND · REPAIR CANNOT BE IMPOSED · s9 SATISFACTORY QUALITY

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Tom pays a plumber £800 to install a new bathroom. The plumber installs the shower incorrectly — water leaks behind the tiles, causing damage. The plumber says the job is finished and refuses to return. Advise Tom under the CRA 2015.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer: Tom has a clear claim under s.49 CRA 2015: the trader must perform a service with reasonable care and skill. Incorrectly installing a shower resulting in water damage is a plain breach of this implied term. Tom's primary remedy is repeat performance under s.55 CRA 2015: he can require the plumber to redo or remedy the defective work within a reasonable time and without significant inconvenience to Tom. The plumber cannot refuse — this is a statutory right. If the plumber refuses or repeat performance is impossible or would take unreasonably long, Tom moves to the second-tier remedy: price reduction under s.56 CRA 2015, which can be up to 100% of the price paid (i.e. Tom may recover the full £800 if the work is entirely worthless). Tom may also claim damages for the consequential damage to the tiles and surrounding area caused by the defective installation. He should document the defects, write to the plumber asserting his s.55 right, and set a reasonable deadline for remediation.
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.summary_line: CRA 2015 s49 · REASONABLE CARE AND SKILL · BREACH · s55 REPEAT PERFORMANCE · s56 PRICE REDUCTION · UP TO 100%

Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.front: Going deeper: Does the timing of notice rule in Olley v Marlborough Court reflect a principled approach to exclusion clauses — or is it a fiction?
Exclusion and Limitation Clauses.answer:
Olley v Marlborough Court Hotel [1949] established that a notice in a hotel bedroom could not incorporate an exclusion clause because the contract was formed at the reception desk — before the guest entered the room. This reflects the general rule: terms must be communicated before or at the time of contracting. The rule is intuitively appealing but faces several critiques:

1. Fiction of assent: in neither Olley nor Thornton v Shoe Lane Parking [1971] did the guest actually read or agree to the terms — the 'time of contracting' rule simply determines which set of unread terms prevails. The underlying fiction is that parties contract on terms they have not read, merely because notice was given at the 'right' moment.
2. Course of dealing alternative: where parties have dealt many times on the same terms, the timing rule is displaced — the term is incorporated by course of dealing even if not communicated at the specific contracting moment (Spurling v Bradshaw [1956]).
3. UCTA 1977 and CRA 2015: statutory controls now do more work than the common law incorporation rules in practice. A clause incorporated by signature or notice may still be void (s.2
1. UCTA — personal injury) or unfair (s.62 CRA 2015 — consumer contracts).

The deeper question is whether incorporation doctrine is doing meaningful independent work in an era of statutory controls — or whether it survives as a useful first filter before the reasonableness/fairness analysis. McMeel argues the two analyses should be integrated.

Misrepresentation.front: What are the elements of an actionable misrepresentation?
Misrepresentation.answer:
An actionable misrepresentation requires:

1. a false statement of existing fact (not opinion, not future intention — though a statement of opinion may be a misrepresentation if the maker did not hold that opinion: Smith v Land & House Property Corp [1884])
2. made by one contracting party to the other
3. which induced the claimant to enter the contract (was material and actually relied upon).

The misrepresentation need not be the sole inducement (Edgington v Fitzmaurice [1885]).
Misrepresentation.summary_line: MISREPRESENTATION · FALSE FACT · INDUCEMENT · RELIANCE · OPINION CAN BE FACT · EDGINGTON · SMITH v LAND

Misrepresentation.front: What are the three types of misrepresentation and how are they distinguished?
Misrepresentation.answer:
1. Fraudulent: false statement made knowingly, without belief in its truth, or recklessly as to its truth (Derry v Peek [1889]).
2. Negligent (s.2
1. Misrepresentation Act 1967): made without reasonable grounds for belief in its truth — the representor is presumed negligent unless they prove reasonable grounds.
3. Innocent: honestly believed to be true and there were reasonable grounds for that belief.

Classification is crucial because remedies differ significantly.
Misrepresentation.summary_line: FRAUDULENT · NEGLIGENT s2(1) MA 1967 · INNOCENT · DERRY v PEEK · REMEDIES DIFFER

Misrepresentation.front: What remedies are available for fraudulent misrepresentation?
Misrepresentation.answer:
For fraudulent misrepresentation:

1. rescission (available as of right, subject to bars)
2. damages in the tort of deceit — the claimant recovers all losses directly caused by the fraud, even if unforeseeable (Doyle v Olby [1969]; Smith New Court Securities v Citibank [1997]).

Damages are assessed as at the date of the transaction (the date of acquisition), allowing the claimant to recover all losses flowing from the fraud regardless of subsequent market movements. This is more generous than the contract measure and avoids any foreseeability limit.
Misrepresentation.summary_line: FRAUD · RESCISSION + DECEIT DAMAGES · ALL DIRECT LOSS · NO REMOTENESS · DATE OF TRANSACTION · DOYLE v OLBY · SMITH NEW COURT

Misrepresentation.front:
What remedies are available under s.2

1. and s.2
2. of the Misrepresentation Act 1967?
Misrepresentation.answer:
s.2(1) (negligent misrepresentation): the representee may claim damages as if the misrepresentation were fraudulent — the 'fiction of fraud' applies so the broader deceit measure applies rather than the contract measure (Royscot Trust v Rogerson [1991]).

⚠️ Royscot has been criticised but remains authority. The representor bears the burden of proving reasonable grounds. Rescission also available. s.2(2): where the representee would otherwise be entitled to rescind, the court may award damages in lieu of rescission for non-fraudulent misrepresentation (discretionary).
Misrepresentation.summary_line: s2(1) MA · DAMAGES AS IF FRAUD · FICTION OF FRAUD · ROYSCOT ⚠️ · s2(2) DAMAGES IN LIEU OF RESCISSION

Misrepresentation.front: What are the bars to rescission for misrepresentation?
Misrepresentation.answer:
Rescission is lost where:

1. Affirmation — claimant, with knowledge of the misrepresentation, elects to affirm the contract
2. Lapse of time — delay may bar rescission, particularly for innocent misrepresentation (Leaf v International Galleries [1950]: 5-year delay barred rescission for innocent misrepresentation)
3. Third party rights — an innocent third party has acquired rights in the subject matter
4. Restitutio in integrum impossible — parties cannot be restored to their original positions. Additionally, the court may award damages in lieu under s.2
2. MA 1967.
Misrepresentation.summary_line: BARS: AFFIRMATION · LAPSE OF TIME · THIRD PARTY RIGHTS · RESTITUTIO IMPOSSIBLE · LEAF v GALLERIES

Misrepresentation.front: For each scenario, identify the type of misrepresentation and the primary remedy available: (a) A car dealer tells a buyer the car has done 20,000 miles. He genuinely believes this, having been told so by a previous owner, but it has actually done 80,000. (b) A seller tells a buyer land is not subject to any restrictive covenants, knowing this is false. (c) A seller honestly but carelessly states a machine produces 500 units/hour when it produces 300.
Misrepresentation.answer:
(a) Innocent misrepresentation — the dealer had reasonable grounds for his belief. Primary remedy: rescission (subject to bars); no right to damages under s.2

1. MA 1967 because the dealer can prove reasonable grounds. Court may award damages in lieu under s.2(2). (b) Fraudulent misrepresentation (Derry v Peek [1889]) — known to be false. Remedies: rescission as of right + damages in deceit (all direct losses from date of transaction, no remoteness limit: Doyle v Olby; Smith New Court). (c) Negligent misrepresentation under s.2
1. MA 1967 — carelessly made without reasonable grounds.

Remedies: rescission + damages assessed as if fraudulent (fiction of fraud: Royscot Trust v Rogerson

⚠️).
Misrepresentation.summary_line: INNOCENT · FRAUDULENT · NEGLIGENT s2(1) · REMEDIES DIFFER · RESCISSION · DECEIT DAMAGES · FICTION OF FRAUD

Misrepresentation.front: In each scenario, identify which bar to rescission applies: (a) A buyer discovers a misrepresentation in a painting purchase but continues to display the painting for 8 months before seeking rescission. (b) A buyer discovers a misrepresentation but sells the goods on to an innocent third party before rescinding. (c) A buyer of a business discovers the accounts were misstated but the business has been traded for 2 years, assets mixed with other assets, and staff restructured.
Misrepresentation.answer: (a) Affirmation — continuing to use/display the goods after discovering the misrepresentation amounts to an election to affirm (Long v Lloyd [1958]). Rescission barred. (b) Third party rights — once an innocent third party acquires rights in the subject matter, the representee can no longer rescind (Car & Universal Finance v Caldwell [1965]). Rescission barred. (c) Restitutio in integrum impossible — after 2 years of trading, the parties cannot be restored to their pre-contractual positions; rescission barred. In all three cases, damages under s.2(1) MA 1967 may still be available if the misrepresentation was negligent or fraudulent.
Misrepresentation.summary_line: AFFIRMATION · LONG v LLOYD · THIRD PARTY · CAR & UNIVERSAL · RESTITUTIO IMPOSSIBLE · DAMAGES STILL POSSIBLE

Misrepresentation.front: A trader sells a used laptop to a consumer, saying 'the battery lasts 10 hours.' It lasts 2 hours. The consumer is still within 30 days of purchase. What is the consumer's best legal position and primary remedy?
Misrepresentation.answer: The consumer's best position combines statutory and misrepresentation claims: Under CRA 2015 s.11, goods must match their description. A specific battery life claim is part of the description — the laptop does not match it. Under s.20 CRA 2015, the consumer has a short-term right to reject within 30 days and claim a full refund. This is the strongest and simplest remedy — no need to prove fault, simply that the goods do not conform. Additionally, the statement 'battery lasts 10 hours' is a false statement of existing fact (not mere opinion) that induced the purchase — an actionable misrepresentation. If negligent (s.2(1) MA 1967): damages + rescission available. However, the consumer should lead with the s.20 CRA 2015 right to reject: it is strict liability (no need to prove misrepresentation), the refund is automatic within 30 days, and it is the most certain remedy. The SBA answer: the consumer will succeed under s.20 CRA 2015 and is entitled to a full refund because the goods do not conform to their description (s.11 CRA 2015), and the 30-day short-term right to reject has not expired.
Misrepresentation.summary_line: CRA 2015 s11 · DESCRIPTION · s20 RIGHT TO REJECT · 30 DAYS · FULL REFUND · ALSO MISREP · LEAD WITH CRA

Misrepresentation.front: Can a party exclude or restrict liability for misrepresentation, and what test applies?
Misrepresentation.answer:
Under s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 (as amended by UCTA 1977), any term which purports to exclude or restrict: (a) liability for misrepresentation; or (b) any remedy available to the other party for misrepresentation, is of no effect except to the extent that it satisfies the reasonableness test under s.11 UCTA 1977. This applies whether the contract is B2B or B2C — s.3 MA 1967 is not limited to consumer contracts. The reasonableness test is assessed at the time the contract was made (not the time of breach). Key cases: Walker v Boyle [1982] — a clause in a property sale contract excluding liability for misrepresentation failed the reasonableness test; the clause was standard form and the purchaser had no real opportunity to negotiate it. Contrast: Watford Electronics v Sanderson [2001] — in a negotiated B2B contract between experienced parties of equal bargaining strength, an exclusion clause satisfied the reasonableness test.

⚠️ Exam trap: a 'whole agreement' clause or 'no representation' clause may amount to an attempt to exclude misrepresentation liability — it must still satisfy s.3 MA 1967 / UCTA s.11.
Misrepresentation.summary_line: MA 1967 s3 · EXCLUDE MISREP LIABILITY · UCTA s11 REASONABLENESS · B2B AND B2C · WALKER v BOYLE · WHOLE AGREEMENT CLAUSE

Misrepresentation.front: What types of statement can constitute an actionable misrepresentation — and what cannot?
Misrepresentation.answer:
An actionable misrepresentation must be a false statement of existing fact. Statement of opinion: generally not actionable — a representor who honestly holds the opinion stated has not misrepresented (Bisset v Wilkinson [1927]: seller's belief that land could support 2,000 sheep was opinion, not fact).
