In White & Carter (Councils) Ltd v McGregor [1962] HL, the House of Lords (by 3–2 majority) held that an innocent party who elects to affirm a repudiated contract may continue to perform without the co-operation of the repudiating party and sue for the full contract price when performance is complete. White & Carter continued displaying McGregor's advertisements after McGregor cancelled, without any need for McGregor's involvement, and recovered the full price. Limits — the innocent party cannot affirm and continue where:

1. Performance requires the co-operation of the repudiating party (the majority noted this).
2. The innocent party has no legitimate interest in performing rather than accepting the repudiation and claiming damages (Lord Reid's dictum — e.g. where damages would fully compensate and continuing performance is unreasonable).

The 'legitimate interest' qualification has been developed in later cases (Clea Shipping v Bulk Oil [1984]: no legitimate interest if damages are an adequate remedy and affirmation is unreasonable). Contrast the duty to mitigate: a party who accepts repudiation must mitigate, but a party who affirms and performs need not.
Breach.summary_line: WHITE & CARTER v McGREGOR · AFFIRM · CONTINUE PERFORMANCE · FULL PRICE · LEGITIMATE INTEREST · NO CO-OPERATION LIMIT · CLEA SHIPPING

Breach.issue: Whether an innocent party can sue for damages immediately upon anticipatory repudiation, without waiting until the date fixed for performance.
Breach.rule: Where a party repudiates a contract before the performance date (anticipatory breach), the innocent party may elect to accept the repudiation and bring an immediate action for damages — without waiting for the performance date to arrive.
Breach.application: De La Tour engaged Hochster as a courier for a European tour commencing 1 June. On 11 May, De La Tour wrote saying he had changed his mind and would not be needing Hochster's services. Hochster sued on 22 May — before the contract was due to commence. The court held he was entitled to sue immediately, without waiting until 1 June. Acceptance of the repudiation created an immediate cause of action.
Breach.conclusion: Anticipatory breach established. The innocent party may accept the repudiation immediately and sue at once, or affirm and wait (at the risk that a subsequent frustrating event may discharge the contract: Avery v Bowden).
Breach.summary_line: HOCHSTER v DE LA TOUR · ANTICIPATORY BREACH · SUE IMMEDIATELY · DO NOT WAIT · ELECT: ACCEPT OR AFFIRM · AVERY v BOWDEN RISK

Breach.issue: Whether an innocent party who elects to affirm a repudiated contract may continue to perform and recover the full contract price, without the co-operation of the repudiating party.
Breach.rule: An innocent party who affirms a contract following anticipatory repudiation may, where performance does not require the other party's co-operation, continue to perform and recover the full contract price — provided they have a legitimate interest in performing rather than claiming damages.
Breach.application: McGregor cancelled an advertising contract immediately after making it. White & Carter, who displayed advertisements on litter bins and did not need McGregor's involvement to continue, chose to affirm and displayed the adverts for the full contract period. The House of Lords (3–2 majority) held they were entitled to the full contract price. Lord Reid noted a qualification: affirmation is impermissible where the innocent party has no legitimate interest in performing (e.g. where damages fully compensate and continuing performance is unreasonable).
Breach.conclusion:
Innocent party may affirm and perform without the repudiating party's co-operation, recovering the full price. Subject to:

1. no requirement for the other party's co-operation
2. a legitimate interest in performance beyond merely avoiding the duty to mitigate.

Developed in Clea Shipping v Bulk Oil [1984].
Breach.summary_line: WHITE & CARTER · AFFIRM · CONTINUE WITHOUT CO-OPERATION · FULL PRICE · LEGITIMATE INTEREST QUALIFICATION · CLEA SHIPPING

Breach.front: In January, GlobalCo contracts to supply machinery to FactoryLtd in June. In March, GlobalCo writes to FactoryLtd: 'We will not be supplying the machinery in June.' FactoryLtd immediately sources replacement machinery from a third party at a higher cost and claims the price difference from GlobalCo. GlobalCo argues FactoryLtd should have waited until June. Advise FactoryLtd.
Breach.answer: FactoryLtd acted correctly. GlobalCo's letter in March was an anticipatory breach — a clear indication before the performance date that it would not perform (Hochster v De La Tour [1853]). FactoryLtd was entitled to accept the repudiation immediately and sue for damages without waiting until June. By accepting the repudiation, FactoryLtd was also under a duty to mitigate its loss — sourcing replacement machinery promptly was exactly the mitigation required. The price difference between the original contract price and the cost of the replacement machinery is the measure of FactoryLtd's loss and is recoverable, provided the replacement was purchased at a reasonable market price (British Westinghouse v Underground Electric Railways [1912]). GlobalCo cannot complain that FactoryLtd did not wait until June — the whole point of anticipatory breach is that the innocent party need not wait.
Breach.summary_line: ANTICIPATORY BREACH · HOCHSTER · ACCEPT IMMEDIATELY · MITIGATE · REPLACEMENT COST RECOVERABLE · NO NEED TO WAIT

Breach.front: In February, TradeCo repudiates a contract with SupplyCo due to be performed in May. SupplyCo decides not to accept the repudiation and affirms the contract. In April, the contract becomes illegal due to new government sanctions. SupplyCo now has no remedy at all. Explain why and what SupplyCo should have done.
Breach.answer:
This mirrors Avery v Bowden [1855]. When TradeCo repudiated in February, SupplyCo had a choice:

1. accept the repudiation immediately and sue for damages; or
2. affirm the contract and wait for the May performance date.

By affirming, SupplyCo kept the contract alive — but that kept it alive for all purposes. The government sanctions in April frustrated the contract before the May performance date. A frustrated contract is discharged automatically, releasing both parties. Critically, by affirming after the February repudiation, SupplyCo lost the right to sue on the original breach — the subsequent frustration wiped out TradeCo's liability. SupplyCo is left with no remedy. What SupplyCo should have done: accepted the repudiation in February and commenced a claim for damages immediately — before the frustrating event in April. The lesson: affirming following anticipatory breach is a gamble. If a supervening event subsequently discharges the contract, the right to sue for the breach is lost.
Breach.summary_line: AVERY v BOWDEN · AFFIRM = GAMBLE · FRUSTRATION DEFEATS CLAIM · LOST REMEDY · SHOULD HAVE ACCEPTED IN FEBRUARY

Breach.front: A lead singer is contracted to perform at 10 concerts over 3 months. She misses the first concert without explanation. The promoter immediately purports to terminate the entire contract and books a replacement. The singer claims the promoter has himself repudiated the contract. Advise the promoter.
Breach.answer: The promoter's termination is risky and may itself be a repudiatory breach. Whether one missed concert justifies termination of the whole contract depends on how the term is classified. If attendance at each concert is a condition, any breach — however minor — justifies termination. If it is an innominate term, the promoter may only terminate if the breach deprives him of substantially the whole benefit of the 10-concert contract (Hong Kong Fir [1962]). Missing one concert out of ten is unlikely to satisfy this threshold. By purporting to terminate the whole contract, the promoter may have committed a repudiatory breach, entitling the singer to accept that repudiation and claim damages. Compare Poussard v Spiers [1876] (lead actress missing opening night — condition) with Bettini v Gye [1876] (rehearsals — warranty). A single missed concert is more analogous to Bettini. The promoter should have affirmed and claimed damages for the missed concert only.
Breach.summary_line: REPUDIATORY BREACH · CONDITION vs INNOMINATE · HK FIR · ONE OF TEN CONCERTS · PROMOTER RISKS OWN REPUDIATION · POUSSARD · BETTINI

Breach.front: SportsCo contracts with StadiumLtd to display its logo on stadium hoardings for one year. After two months, StadiumLtd repudiates the contract. SportsCo does not need StadiumLtd's involvement to continue displaying the logo — the hoardings are already in place. SportsCo chooses to affirm and continues displaying the logo for the remaining 10 months, then claims the full annual fee. StadiumLtd argues SportsCo should have accepted the repudiation and mitigated. Advise.
Breach.answer: SportsCo's position is supported by White & Carter (Councils) Ltd v McGregor [1962]. The HL held that where an innocent party does not require the repudiating party's co-operation to continue performing, they may affirm and perform, recovering the full contract price. Here, the hoardings are already in place — SportsCo can continue displaying the logo without any involvement from StadiumLtd. The co-operation requirement is satisfied. The duty to mitigate does not apply to a party who affirms — it only applies where the innocent party accepts the repudiation. However, StadiumLtd may argue SportsCo had no legitimate interest in performing rather than accepting the repudiation and claiming damages (Lord Reid's qualification in White & Carter; Clea Shipping v Bulk Oil [1984]). If damages would have fully compensated SportsCo and continuing performance served no purpose beyond recovering the contract price, the court might find no legitimate interest. On balance: if the logo display had genuine business value to SportsCo beyond the fee (e.g. brand exposure), the legitimate interest test is satisfied and the full fee is recoverable.
Breach.summary_line: WHITE & CARTER · AFFIRM · NO CO-OPERATION NEEDED · FULL PRICE · LEGITIMATE INTEREST · NO DUTY TO MITIGATE ON AFFIRMATION

Discharge by Agreement.front: How is a contract discharged by agreement and what is meant by 'accord and satisfaction'?
Discharge by Agreement.answer:
Discharge by agreement:

1. Bilateral discharge (accord and satisfaction) — both parties agree to release each other from remaining obligations; each party's agreement to release is consideration for the other's (Morris v Baron & Co [1918]).
2. Unilateral waiver — one party waives their right, which requires consideration or promissory estoppel to be binding.
3. Novation — substitution of a new contract, requiring consent of all parties.
4. Condition subsequent — the original contract provides for automatic termination on a future event.
Discharge by Agreement.summary_line: DISCHARGE BY AGREEMENT · ACCORD AND SATISFACTION · BILATERAL · CONSIDERATION · NOVATION · CONDITION SUBSEQUENT

Damages.front: What is the aim of damages in contract law and what are the two main measures?
Damages.answer: The aim of contractual damages is to put the claimant in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed (expectation/loss of bargain measure: Robinson v Harman [1848]). Alternatively, the claimant may claim reliance loss — wasted expenditure incurred in preparation for performance (Anglia Television v Reed [1972]). Reliance loss is useful where expectation loss is difficult to prove. The claimant cannot recover both expectation and reliance loss for the same item of loss.
Damages.summary_line: DAMAGES · EXPECTATION INTEREST · ROBINSON v HARMAN · RELIANCE LOSS · ANGLIA TV · CHOOSE ONE

Damages.front: What is the remoteness test for contractual damages from Hadley v Baxendale [1854]?
Damages.answer:
Damages are recoverable only for loss that:

1. arises naturally from the breach according to the usual course of things (limb 1 — presumed to be within the reasonable contemplation of both parties); OR
2. was within the reasonable contemplation of both parties at the time of contracting as the probable result of the breach (limb 2 — requires actual knowledge of special circumstances).

The test is applied at the time of contracting, not breach. Refined in Victoria Laundry v Newman [1949] and The Heron II [1969]: higher probability required in contract than in tort.
Damages.summary_line: HADLEY v BAXENDALE · NATURAL LOSS · CONTEMPLATION · LIMB 1 & 2 · TIME OF CONTRACTING · VICTORIA LAUNDRY

Damages.front: What is the duty to mitigate and how does it affect a damages claim?
Damages.answer: A claimant cannot recover damages for losses that could reasonably have been avoided (British Westinghouse Electric v Underground Electric Railways [1912]). The claimant must take reasonable steps to reduce their loss — they need not take exceptional steps or risk their own funds. If the claimant fails to mitigate, damages are reduced by the amount that could have been avoided. If mitigation steps go further and improve the claimant's position, gains may be offset against the damages award.
Damages.summary_line: MITIGATION · REASONABLY AVOID LOSS · BRITISH WESTINGHOUSE · NO EXCEPTIONAL STEPS · REDUCES IF FAILS

Damages.front: What is the distinction between liquidated damages and penalty clauses and what is the current test following Cavendish Square v Makdessi [2015]?
Damages.answer:
Liquidated damages: a genuine pre-estimate of loss agreed at contracting — enforceable even if actual loss differs. Penalty clause: formerly, a sum designed to punish breach — traditionally unenforceable. Post-Cavendish Square v Makdessi [2015] (Supreme Court): a clause is a penalty only if it is out of all proportion to any legitimate interest the innocent party has in enforcing the primary obligation. The test focuses on legitimate interests, not whether the sum is a genuine pre-estimate.

⚠️ Recommend verifying any further developments.
Damages.summary_line: LIQUIDATED DAMAGES = ENFORCEABLE · PENALTY = DISPROPORTIONATE · LEGITIMATE INTEREST · CAVENDISH ⚠️

Damages.front: When can a claimant recover damages for non-financial loss (loss of amenity / consumer surplus) in contract?
Damages.answer: Damages for non-financial loss may be recovered where the purpose of the contract was to provide pleasure, relaxation, peace of mind, or freedom from distress, and this was known to both parties (Jarvis v Swans Tours [1973]: holiday package — award for loss of enjoyment; Ruxley Electronics v Forsyth [1996]: swimming pool built to wrong depth — £2,500 for loss of amenity even though no diminution in market value). Courts are reluctant to award this in purely commercial contracts. Amounts tend to be modest and discretionary.
Damages.summary_line: NON-FINANCIAL LOSS · LOSS OF AMENITY · PLEASURE/PEACE OF MIND · JARVIS v SWANS TOURS · RUXLEY v FORSYTH

Damages.front: A mill owner sends his broken crankshaft to a carrier for repair. He tells the carrier: 'This is our only crankshaft and the mill will be shut until it is returned.' The carrier delays delivery and the mill loses two weeks' profit. Applying Hadley v Baxendale, can the mill owner recover lost profits?
Damages.answer: Yes, on these facts — but the result in Hadley v Baxendale itself was the opposite. In the original case, the mill owner did NOT communicate that the mill was at a standstill. The court held that lost profits failed limb 1 (a carrier would not naturally assume the mill was shut) and also failed limb 2 (no special knowledge communicated). On our varied facts, the mill owner expressly communicated to the carrier that the mill would be shut until the crankshaft was returned. This brings the loss within limb 2: the carrier had actual knowledge of the special circumstances at the time of contracting. The lost profits were within the reasonable contemplation of both parties as a probable result of delay. Recovery is therefore available. Contrast Victoria Laundry v Newman [1949]: ordinary laundry profits recoverable (limb 1); exceptional government contracts not recoverable (limb 2 — not communicated).
Damages.summary_line: HADLEY LIMB 2 · SPECIAL KNOWLEDGE COMMUNICATED · CRANKSHAFT · LOST PROFITS RECOVERABLE · VICTORIA LAUNDRY · COMMUNICATE AT CONTRACTING

Damages.front: What is quantum meruit, and when is a restitutionary claim for unjust enrichment available in a contractual context?
Damages.answer:
Quantum meruit ('as much as he has earned') is a restitutionary remedy allowing a party to recover a reasonable sum for work done or services rendered, where no fixed price was agreed or where the contract has been discharged before completion. It is based on unjust enrichment — the defendant has received a benefit at the claimant's expense with no legal basis to retain it without payment. Key situations:

1. Void contract: where a contract is void (e.g. for mistake or lack of capacity), a party who has performed may recover a reasonable sum on quantum meruit rather than the contract price.
2. Frustrated contract: under s.1
3. Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, where one party has conferred a valuable benefit on the other before frustration, the court may award a just sum (not necessarily the contract price). BP Exploration Co v Hunt [1982] established that the 'valuable benefit' is assessed as the end product received by the defendant, not the cost of performance.
3. Contract discharged by breach: an innocent party who has partly performed before the other repudiates may claim quantum meruit for the value of work done.
4. No price agreed: where parties perform without agreeing a price, quantum meruit implies a reasonable sum (s.15 Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982; s.51 CRA 2015 for consumers).
Damages.summary_line: QUANTUM MERUIT · UNJUST ENRICHMENT · VOID CONTRACT · LRFCA 1943 s1(3) · BP v HUNT · VALUABLE BENEFIT · REASONABLE SUM

Damages.front: Hadley v Baxendale [1854]
Damages.issue: What principles determine whether a particular head of loss is recoverable for breach of contract.
Damages.rule:
Damages are recoverable only for losses that:

1. arise naturally from the breach in the usual course of things (limb 1 — presumed to be within parties' contemplation); or
2. were within the reasonable contemplation of both parties at the time of contracting as the probable result of the breach (limb 2 — requires actual knowledge of special circumstances).
Damages.application: A mill's crankshaft broke and was sent to the defendants (carrier) for repair. The carrier delayed delivery. The mill stood idle, losing profits. The carrier did not know the mill had no spare crankshaft. The court held: lost profits were not recoverable — they did not arise naturally (limb 1: a carrier would not assume the mill was shut) and the carrier had no special knowledge of the mill's situation (limb 2).
Damages.conclusion: Remoteness rule for contractual damages established. Two-limb test applied at time of contracting. The test was refined in Victoria Laundry and The Heron II.
Damages.summary_line: HADLEY v BAXENDALE · REMOTENESS · LIMB 1 NATURAL LOSS · LIMB 2 SPECIAL KNOWLEDGE · TIME OF CONTRACTING

Damages.front: Victoria Laundry (Windsor) Ltd v Newman Industries Ltd [1949]
Damages.issue: How the Hadley v Baxendale remoteness test applies where different types of loss (ordinary and exceptional profits) are claimed.
Damages.rule: A party recovers losses within the reasonable contemplation of both parties at the time of contracting. Ordinary foreseeable profits are recoverable under limb 1; exceptional or unusual profits are only recoverable under limb 2 if the defendant had actual knowledge of the special circumstances giving rise to them.
Damages.application: Newman contracted to supply a large boiler to Victoria Laundry. Delivery was delayed by five months. Victoria Laundry claimed for: (a) profits from ordinary laundry business lost during delay; (b) profits from a highly lucrative dyeing contract with the Ministry of Supply. The Court of Appeal held: (a) recoverable — Newman knew a laundry needed a boiler to operate; (b) not recoverable — Newman had no knowledge of the government contract.
Damages.conclusion: Two types of profit treated differently. Ordinary profits = limb 1. Exceptional profits = limb 2 (need special knowledge communicated at time of contracting). A clear practical application of Hadley.
Damages.summary_line: VICTORIA LAUNDRY · ORDINARY PROFITS = LIMB 1 · EXCEPTIONAL PROFITS = LIMB 2 · MINISTRY CONTRACT · KNOWLEDGE AT CONTRACTING

Damages.front: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996]
Damages.issue: Whether a claimant can recover damages for non-financial loss (loss of amenity) where there is no diminution in market value, and whether the cost of reinstatement is an appropriate measure.
Damages.rule: Damages for loss of amenity or consumer surplus are available where a contract is designed to provide pleasure or a personal benefit. The cost of reinstatement is not always the appropriate measure — it must be reasonable and proportionate.
Damages.application: Ruxley contracted to build a swimming pool for Forsyth to a depth of 7 feet 6 inches. It was built to 6 feet (safe but not as specified). There was no diminution in market value. The House of Lords refused cost of reinstatement (disproportionate at £21,560 for a negligible difference) but awarded £2,500 for loss of amenity — the lost pleasure and personal value of the deeper pool.
Damages.conclusion: Loss of amenity damages are recoverable in consumer-type contracts even without financial loss. Cost of reinstatement not always appropriate — must be proportionate. Modest but principled award.
Damages.summary_line: RUXLEY v FORSYTH · LOSS OF AMENITY · £2,500 · NO MARKET VALUE LOSS · CONSUMER SURPLUS · REINSTATEMENT DISPROPORTIONATE

Damages.front: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Makdessi; ParkingEye Ltd v Beavis [2015]
Damages.issue: What is the correct test for determining whether a contractual clause providing for a remedy on breach is an unenforceable penalty clause.
Damages.rule: A clause is not a penalty merely because it exceeds a genuine pre-estimate of loss. A clause is only an unenforceable penalty if it imposes a detriment on the breaching party that is out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party in enforcing the primary obligation.
Damages.application: Cavendish: Makdessi sold his advertising business and included non-compete covenants. Breach triggered clauses reducing payment and requiring share transfer. SC held: not a penalty — Cavendish had a legitimate interest in the goodwill of the business beyond mere financial compensation. ParkingEye: an £85 overstaying charge held enforceable — legitimate interest in managing car park flow.
Damages.conclusion: Old genuine pre-estimate test replaced. New test: legitimate interest + proportionality. Significantly expanded scope for agreed remedies clauses. Marks a liberalisation from the traditional penalty doctrine.
Damages.summary_line: CAVENDISH v MAKDESSI · PENALTY CLAUSE · LEGITIMATE INTEREST · PROPORTIONALITY · PRE-ESTIMATE TEST ABANDONED · SC 2015

Damages.front: A printer is hired to produce 10,000 brochures for a product launch on 1 March. The printer delivers late on 15 March. The client claims: (a) £5,000 lost profit from delayed sales; and (b) £80,000 lost profit from a specific distribution contract with a major retailer, which the client had not told the printer about. Advise on recoverability.
Damages.answer: Applying Hadley v Baxendale [1854]: (a) £5,000 lost profit from delayed sales: likely recoverable under limb 1 — a printer delivering brochures for a product launch would naturally expect that delay would cause some loss of sales and profit. This arises naturally from the breach. (b) £80,000 from the undisclosed retailer contract: not recoverable under limb 2. The printer had no knowledge of the specific distribution contract at the time of contracting. This is special loss arising from particular circumstances that were never communicated. For limb 2 loss, the defendant must have actual knowledge of the special circumstances at the time of contracting (Victoria Laundry v Newman [1949]). Since the client never disclosed the retailer arrangement, the printer is not liable for this loss. The client should have communicated the importance of the retailer contract at the outset to bring it within limb 2.
Damages.summary_line: HADLEY v BAXENDALE · LIMB 1: NATURAL LOSS RECOVERABLE · LIMB 2: UNDISCLOSED = NOT RECOVERABLE · COMMUNICATE AT CONTRACTING

Damages.front: RecruiterCo wrongfully terminates an employee's contract. The employee, who was earning £4,000 per month, does nothing for six months before finding equivalent work. She claims six months' lost salary (£24,000). RecruiterCo argues she should have found work sooner. Advise on the quantum of damages.
Damages.answer: The employee is under a duty to mitigate her loss (British Westinghouse Electric v Underground Electric Railways [1912]). She must take reasonable steps to reduce her loss — she is not required to take exceptional steps or accept work well below her previous level, but she must make reasonable efforts. If the court finds she should reasonably have found equivalent work within, say, three months, her damages will be limited to three months' salary (£12,000) — she cannot recover for the additional three months she sat idle. The burden is on RecruiterCo to prove the failure to mitigate and what steps would have been reasonable. The employee should be prepared to show evidence of active job-seeking. If six months was genuinely the minimum time to find equivalent work in the market, the full £24,000 is recoverable. The court assesses what was reasonable in the circumstances.
Damages.summary_line: DUTY TO MITIGATE · BRITISH WESTINGHOUSE · REASONABLE STEPS · BURDEN ON DEFENDANT · IDLE PERIOD REDUCED · REASONABLE JOB SEARCH

Damages.front: A couple pay £8,000 for a luxury holiday described as a '5-star all-inclusive Caribbean resort'. On arrival they find the hotel is 3-star, under construction, and the meals are limited. They cut the holiday short after 3 days. They claim damages including disappointment and distress. The tour operator argues damages for distress are not recoverable.
Damages.answer: The tour operator is wrong. Damages for disappointment, distress, and loss of enjoyment are recoverable where the purpose of the contract was to provide pleasure, relaxation, or freedom from distress — and the other party was aware of this (Jarvis v Swans Tours [1973]: a disappointing ski holiday gave rise to damages for loss of enjoyment). A luxury holiday contract is paradigmatically a contract for pleasure — both parties understood its purpose. The couple are entitled to damages for: (a) the diminution in value of the holiday (difference between what was promised and what was provided); and (b) loss of enjoyment and distress caused by the breach. They should also recover wasted costs for the 7 days not taken and any reasonable additional expenses incurred. The tour operator cannot restrict recovery to pure financial loss when the contractual purpose was personal enjoyment.
Damages.summary_line: NON-FINANCIAL LOSS · JARVIS v SWANS TOURS · LOSS OF ENJOYMENT · DISTRESS RECOVERABLE · PURPOSE = PLEASURE · DIMINUTION + ENJOYMENT

Damages.front: A commercial contract provides: 'In the event of late payment, the defaulting party shall pay £50,000 per day regardless of actual loss.' A party pays 2 days late. Actual loss to the other side was £500. The paying party argues the clause is an unenforceable penalty. Advise.
Damages.answer:
Post-Cavendish Square v Makdessi [2015] (SC), the test is no longer whether the clause is a genuine pre-estimate of loss. The clause is only an unenforceable penalty if it imposes a detriment out of all proportion to any legitimate interest the innocent party has in enforcing the primary obligation. The court asks: what legitimate interest did the innocent party have in timely payment? That interest may include: cash flow protection; avoiding the cost of credit; operational planning. However, £50,000 per day against actual loss of £500 is a 100:1 ratio. It is very difficult to identify a legitimate interest that could justify this level of detriment for a 2-day delay. On these facts, the clause is almost certainly a penalty under the Cavendish test — the detriment is grossly disproportionate to any legitimate interest. The innocent party is limited to recovering actual loss of £500.

⚠️ The Cavendish test is relatively new and context-specific — outcomes may vary by contract type and commercial context.
Damages.summary_line: PENALTY CLAUSE · CAVENDISH v MAKDESSI · LEGITIMATE INTEREST · PROPORTIONALITY · 100:1 RATIO · LIKELY PENALTY · £500 ONLY ⚠️

Damages.front: Ahmed is hired to refurbish a restaurant for £40,000. After completing 60% of the work, the restaurant owner repudiates the contract without justification. Ahmed has not agreed a price for partial completion. What can Ahmed recover?
Damages.answer:
Ahmed has two potential claims:

1. Damages for breach of contract: the restaurant owner has repudiated — Ahmed may accept the repudiation and claim damages. He can recover his expectation loss: the profit he would have made on the full contract, plus wasted costs, subject to his duty to mitigate.
2. Quantum meruit — restitutionary claim: where a contract is discharged by the other party's breach and the innocent party has partly performed, they may alternatively claim a reasonable sum for the work done (quantum meruit) rather than contract damages.

This is useful if Ahmed would have made a loss on the contract, or if proving expectation loss is difficult. The reasonable sum is assessed by reference to the market value of the work done — not necessarily 60% of £40,000 (it could be more or less depending on market rates). Ahmed should choose whichever remedy gives the better recovery. He cannot claim both contractual damages and quantum meruit for the same loss. He must also mitigate — taking on replacement work if reasonably available.
Damages.summary_line: QUANTUM MERUIT · PARTIAL PERFORMANCE · REPUDIATION · REASONABLE SUM · OR DAMAGES · MITIGATE · CANNOT CLAIM BOTH

Damages.front: Going deeper: Expectation versus reliance damages — which measure is more theoretically justified, and can a claimant always choose?
Damages.answer: Robinson v Harman [1848] established the expectation measure as the primary contractual remedy: the claimant should be placed in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed. Fuller & Perdue (1936) identified three interests: expectation (loss of the bargain), reliance (wasted expenditure), and restitution (return of unjust enrichment). They argued that the expectation measure is paradoxically the hardest to justify — it compensates for a future benefit the claimant never had. The reliance interest (Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972]) compensates for wasted pre-contractual expenditure incurred in reliance on the contract. Reed pulled out of a film; the production company claimed all its wasted expenditure including pre-contract costs. The CA held pre-contract expenditure was recoverable if within the parties' reasonable contemplation. Can a claimant always choose reliance? Not if the contract would have been loss-making: where the defendant can show the claimant would have made a loss even if the contract had been performed, the reliance claim is capped at nil or the expected loss (C&P Haulage v Middleton [1983]). The reliance measure cannot be used to escape a bad bargain. Comparative: civil law systems (French Code Civil art.1231-2) similarly focus on compensating the creditor for loss suffered — both damnum emergens (reliance) and lucrum cessans (expectation). The tension: English courts default to expectation but the availability of reliance gives claimants tactical flexibility — particularly useful when expectation loss is hard to prove.

Damages.front: Going deeper: How far does the recovery of non-pecuniary loss in contract extend beyond Jarvis v Swans Tours — and where are the boundaries?
Damages.answer:
Jarvis v Swans Tours Ltd [1973] was a watershed: the CA (Denning MR) held that damages for disappointment and distress are recoverable in a contract for a holiday — a contract whose purpose is pleasure. This was extended in Ruxley Electronics v Forsyth [1996] (loss of amenity/consumer surplus) and Farley v Skinner [2001] (surveyor's failure to investigate aircraft noise — distress recoverable even though pleasure was only part of the contract purpose). The boundaries established by the HL in Farley v Skinner:

1. The contract must be one where a major or important object was to give pleasure, relaxation, or peace of mind; OR
2. the breach caused physical inconvenience or discomfort.

Distress caused by mere financial loss is not recoverable (Watts v Morrow [1991]: surveyor's negligence — only modest distress award for physical discomfort of living in defective house, not financial anxiety). Tensions: in Addis v Gramophone Co [1909] (HL), damages for the manner of wrongful dismissal (humiliation, distress) were held not recoverable — but this has been eroded by employment law developments (Johnson v Unisys [2003] for unfair dismissal, now governed by statute). Academic debate: is Jarvis/Farley an unprincipled expansion or a legitimate recognition that contracts serve personal as well as financial ends? Treitel favoured a restrictive approach; Poole argues the current framework is broadly satisfactory but the line between 'major object' and 'incidental benefit' remains imprecise.

Damages.front: Going deeper: Did Transfield Shipping Inc v Mercator Shipping (The Achilleas) [2008] change the law of remoteness — or merely restate it?
Damages.answer: In The Achilleas [2008], charterers returned a vessel late, causing the owners to breach a follow-on charterparty at a much lower market rate. The owners claimed the full difference between the follow-on rate and the original rate. The House of Lords held the loss was not recoverable — despite being foreseeable — because the charterers had not assumed responsibility for this type of loss. Lords Hoffmann and Hope introduced an 'assumption of responsibility' test as an alternative or supplement to the Hadley v Baxendale foreseeability test. The controversy: Lords Rodger and Walker (and the majority in subsequent cases) treated the outcome as consistent with Hadley — the loss was simply outside what the parties would reasonably have contemplated as the basis of liability. They rejected assumption of responsibility as a separate test. Academic reaction was significant: Peel and Cartwright argued The Achilleas introduced unjustified uncertainty; Kramer contended assumption of responsibility better explains the results in Victoria Laundry and Hadley itself. Subsequent cases (e.g. Sylvia Shipping [2010]; Supershield v Siemens [2010]) have largely treated The Achilleas as confined to its unusual commercial context — a market where damages for late redelivery were conventionally limited to the daily charter rate. The practical position: Hadley v Baxendale remains the primary test. The Achilleas adds a secondary question in unusual commercial contexts: did the defendant implicitly assume responsibility for this type and scale of loss? Remoteness remains the most intellectually contested area of contract damages.

Damages.front: Going deeper: What is the common law doctrine of total failure of consideration as illustrated by Fibrosa Spolka Akcyjna v Fairbairn [1943] — and how does it interact with the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943?
Damages.answer: Before the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, the common law provided very limited relief when a contract was frustrated after part-payment. The position in Chandler v Webster [1904] was that money already paid was irrecoverable and money due before frustration remained payable — a harsh result. Fibrosa Spolka Akcyjna v Fairbairn Lawson Combe Barbour Ltd [1943]: a Polish buyer paid a deposit for machinery. Germany occupied Poland; the contract was frustrated. The House of Lords overruled Chandler v Webster, holding that where there has been a total failure of consideration — the payer has received absolutely nothing of what they bargained for — the money is recoverable in restitution on the basis of unjust enrichment. Total failure of consideration means the entire contractual benefit has not been received — not merely that it was less than expected. If any part of the consideration has been received, the failure is only partial and Fibrosa does not apply at common law. The problem Fibrosa left: the manufacturer had incurred significant expense in beginning work. A total failure of consideration allowed the buyer to recover the full deposit, leaving the manufacturer uncompensated for its outlay. The 1943 Act responded: s.1(2) allows recovery of money paid where there is a total or partial failure of consideration, subject to the court's discretion to allow the payee to retain expenses. s.1(3) allows recovery of a just sum for a valuable benefit conferred. Academic significance: Fibrosa shifted English law toward a restitutionary model for frustrated contracts — recognising unjust enrichment as a distinct source of obligation alongside contract and tort. Burrows argues Fibrosa is the foundation of the modern law of unjust enrichment in England.

Equitable Remedies.front: When will a court grant specific performance and when will it refuse?
Equitable Remedies.answer: Specific performance (discretionary equitable remedy) granted where: damages are inadequate (unique subject matter — land, unique goods, works of art); the contract is sufficiently certain; the claimant has clean hands; no undue hardship to defendant; no need for constant supervision. Refused where: damages are adequate (most commercial goods: Cohen v Roche [1927]); contracts for personal services (Page One Records v Britton [1968]); uncertainty; unclean hands; constant supervision required (Ryan v Mutual Tontine [1893]). Land contracts almost always attract specific performance.
Equitable Remedies.summary_line: SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · EQUITABLE · DISCRETIONARY · UNIQUE SUBJECT · REFUSED: ADEQUATE DAMAGES · PERSONAL SERVICE

Equitable Remedies.front: What is an injunction in contract law and when is it available to enforce negative contractual obligations?
Equitable Remedies.answer: An injunction orders a party to do something (mandatory) or refrain from doing something (prohibitory). In contract, injunctions are used to enforce negative contractual promises (covenants not to compete, confidentiality obligations). A prohibitory injunction may be granted even where specific performance is unavailable (Warner Bros v Nelson [1937]: Bette Davis restrained from working for others, though not compelled to work for Warner). Interim injunctions: the balance of convenience test applies (American Cyanamid v Ethicon [1975]).
Equitable Remedies.summary_line: INJUNCTION · MANDATORY OR PROHIBITORY · NEGATIVE COVENANTS · WARNER BROS v NELSON · AMERICAN CYANAMID

Equitable Remedies.front: What are the five main bars to obtaining specific performance or an injunction in contract law?
Equitable Remedies.answer:
Equitable remedies are discretionary — the court may refuse them even where the legal right is established. The five key bars:

1. Damages are an adequate remedy: if a damages award would fully compensate the claimant, equity will not intervene — SP is only granted where the subject matter is unique (e.g. land, rare goods: Cohen v Roche [1927] — Hepplewhite chairs not unique enough).
2. Constant supervision: courts will not order SP where performance would require ongoing judicial supervision — Ryan v Mutual Tontine Westminster Chambers Association [1893] (SP refused for a porter's duties requiring daily oversight).
3. Personal services contracts: SP will not be granted to compel personal performance of a contract (e.g. employment, recording artist) — this would infringe personal liberty. An injunction restraining breach of a negative covenant may be granted instead, but only if it does not indirectly compel performance (Page One Records v Britton [1968]).
4. Clean hands: a claimant who has acted inequitably cannot obtain equitable relief — 'he who comes to equity must come with clean hands.'
5. Hardship: the court may refuse SP if granting it would cause severe hardship to the defendant disproportionate to the benefit to the claimant (Patel v Ali [1984] — SP refused for sale of house where defendant had become seriously ill and dependent on local support).
Equitable Remedies.summary_line: SP BARS · DAMAGES ADEQUATE · CONSTANT SUPERVISION RYAN · PERSONAL SERVICES · CLEAN HANDS · HARDSHIP PATEL v ALI

Equitable Remedies.front: Sarah agrees to sell her house to David. She later refuses to complete and accepts a higher offer from another buyer. David wants his house — not damages. Can he obtain specific performance?
Equitable Remedies.answer: Yes — very likely. Specific performance is a discretionary equitable remedy, granted where damages are inadequate. Land is treated as unique in English law — every piece of land has unique characteristics and damages are not considered an adequate substitute (Beswick v Beswick [1968]; specific performance is the standard remedy for breach of a contract for the sale of land). The factors required are met: (a) damages inadequate — David wants this specific house; (b) the contract is sufficiently certain; (c) David has not acted improperly (clean hands). The court would order Sarah to complete the sale at the contract price. If the competing buyer has not yet completed, the specific performance order protects David's equitable interest (which arose on exchange of contracts). If a third party has already received a legal title, the position becomes more complex — but as against Sarah, specific performance is the appropriate and standard remedy.
Equitable Remedies.summary_line: SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · LAND IS UNIQUE · DAMAGES INADEQUATE · DISCRETIONARY · CLEAN HANDS · STANDARD REMEDY FOR LAND

Equitable Remedies.front: A famous chef is contracted exclusively to a restaurant for two years. After six months she leaves to open a competing restaurant. The employer cannot get specific performance of the personal service contract but seeks an injunction to prevent her working for herself. Advise.
Equitable Remedies.answer: Specific performance of a contract of personal service is not available — courts will not compel a person to work for a specific employer (Page One Records v Britton [1968]). However, a prohibitory injunction enforcing a negative contractual covenant may be available, even where specific performance is not (Warner Bros v Nelson [1937]: Bette Davis was restrained from working for a third party during her contract term, even though specific performance to compel her to work for Warner Bros was unavailable). The key question is whether restraining the chef from working for herself would, in effect, compel her to return to the restaurant (which would be indirect specific performance — impermissible). If the chef has other means of earning a living that comply with the injunction, the injunction may be granted. If the only realistic effect of the injunction would be to starve her into returning, it will be refused (Rely-a-Bell Burglar Alarm Co v Eisler [1926]). The employer must draft any injunction narrowly to avoid it operating as indirect compulsion.
Equitable Remedies.summary_line: SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE REFUSED · PERSONAL SERVICE · PROHIBITORY INJUNCTION · NEGATIVE COVENANT · WARNER BROS v NELSON · NOT INDIRECT COMPULSION

Equitable Remedies.front: Dev contracts to sell his rare 1920s signed first edition book to Lena for £12,000. He later refuses to complete. Lena sues for specific performance. Dev argues: (i) the book has since been destroyed in a fire; (ii) even if it existed, damages would be adequate. Advise Lena.
Equitable Remedies.answer: Lena's claim for specific performance faces two obstacles: (i) Destruction of the book: specific performance requires the subject matter to exist — if the book has been destroyed, SP is impossible and the court cannot order it. Lena's remedy is damages for breach of contract (difference in market value, loss of bargain). (ii) Adequacy of damages argument (if the book still existed): this would fail. SP is available where damages are not an adequate remedy — and for unique items, they rarely are. A signed 1920s first edition is unique; no market substitute exists that would put Lena in the same position as performance. Cohen v Roche [1927] denied SP for Hepplewhite chairs on the basis they were ordinary antiques with a market value, not truly unique. A one-of-a-kind signed edition is clearly distinguishable — SP would normally be granted. None of the other bars apply: no constant supervision required; not a personal services contract; no clean hands or hardship issues on these facts. Conclusion: if the book exists, SP is available. If destroyed, Lena claims damages — likely the full market value of the unique item, which may exceed £12,000.
Equitable Remedies.summary_line: SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · UNIQUE ITEM · DAMAGES INADEQUATE · COHEN v ROCHE · SP BARS · DESTRUCTION = DAMAGES ONLY

Privity of Contract.front: What is the doctrine of privity of contract and what are its two key rules?
Privity of Contract.answer:
The doctrine of privity provides:

1. only parties to a contract can sue on it or be bound by it — a third party cannot enforce the contract even if made for their benefit (Tweddle v Atkinson [1861]; Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co v Selfridge [1915])
2. consideration must move from the promisee.

The doctrine was firmly affirmed by the House of Lords in Dunlop v Selfridge. Its harshness has been mitigated by the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 and various common law exceptions.
Privity of Contract.summary_line: PRIVITY · PARTIES ONLY · TWEDDLE v ATKINSON · DUNLOP v SELFRIDGE · MITIGATED BY CRTPA 1999

Privity of Contract.front: Under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, when can a third party enforce a contractual term?
Privity of Contract.answer:
CRTPA 1999, s.1: a third party may enforce a term where:

1. the contract expressly provides that they may (s.1(1)(a)); OR
2. the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party AND the parties did not intend the term to be unenforceable by the third party (s.1(1)(b), s.1(2)).

The third party must be identified by name, class, or description (s.1(3)). The contracting parties may vary or rescind the contract without the third party's consent unless the third party has relied on the term or assented to it (s.2).
Privity of Contract.summary_line: CRTPA 1999 · EXPRESS · PURPORTS TO BENEFIT · IDENTIFIED · RELIED UPON · VARY/RESCIND RESTRICTED

Privity of Contract.front: What are the main common law exceptions to the privity rule?
Privity of Contract.answer:
Common law exceptions:

1. Agency — an agent creates privity between principal and third party
2. Assignment — the benefit (not the burden) of a contract may be assigned
3. Collateral contracts — a contract between A and B supported by A's promise to C (Shanklin Pier v Detel Products [1951])
4. Trusts of promises — a contractual right held on trust for a third party
5. Tort — a duty of care may arise outside contract (Donoghue v Stevenson)
6. Covenants running with land (equity)
7. Statutory exceptions (e.g. Third Parties (Rights Against Insurers) Act; Married Women's Property Act 1882).
Privity of Contract.summary_line: PRIVITY EXCEPTIONS · AGENCY · ASSIGNMENT · COLLATERAL CONTRACT · TRUST · SHANKLIN PIER · TORT

Privity of Contract.front: How do agency and assignment operate as common law exceptions to the privity rule?
Privity of Contract.answer:
Agency: An agent who contracts on behalf of a disclosed principal drops out of the contract — the contract is between the principal and the third party, not the agent. The principal can sue and be sued on the contract even though they did not personally sign it. The agent must have actual or apparent authority. Where the principal is undisclosed, they may still intervene to enforce the contract (Greer v Downs Supply Co [1927]) unless the contract was intended to be personal. Agency is the most commercially significant privity exception — it is the basis of virtually all commercial contracting through representatives, companies, and brokers. Assignment: A party (the assignor) may transfer their contractual rights (not obligations) to a third party (the assignee) by assignment. The assignee then has the right to enforce those rights directly against the other contracting party.

**Key rules:**

1. Only benefits/rights can be assigned — burdens/obligations cannot be unilaterally assigned without the other party's consent (Linden Gardens Trust v Lenesta Sludge [1994]).
2. Notice must be given to the other contracting party (s.136 Law of Property Act 1925 for legal assignment).
3. The assignee takes subject to equities — any defences available against the assignor also apply against the assignee.
Privity of Contract.summary_line: AGENCY · PRINCIPAL ENFORCES · ASSIGNMENT · RIGHTS ONLY · BURDENS CANNOT · NOTICE s136 LPA · SUBJECT TO EQUITIES

Privity of Contract.front: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v Selfridge & Co Ltd [1915]
Privity of Contract.issue: Whether a third party who was not a party to the original contract could enforce a price-maintenance covenant made for their benefit.
Privity of Contract.rule: Only parties to a contract can sue on it or be bound by it. A third party cannot enforce a contractual promise even if the contract was made for their benefit. Consideration must move from the promisee.
Privity of Contract.application: Dunlop sold tyres to Dew (a wholesaler) on terms that included a price-maintenance covenant and that Dew would impose the same covenant on any retailer. Dew sold to Selfridge on the same terms. Selfridge sold below the list price. Dunlop sued Selfridge. The House of Lords held: Dunlop was not a party to the contract between Dew and Selfridge and had provided no consideration to Selfridge. Dunlop could not enforce the covenant.
Privity of Contract.conclusion: Privity rule confirmed at the highest level. Third party cannot enforce even if the contract was made for their benefit. Now substantially mitigated by the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999.
Privity of Contract.summary_line: DUNLOP v SELFRIDGE · PRIVITY · THIRD PARTY CANNOT ENFORCE · NO CONSIDERATION · HL · CRTPA 1999 NOW MITIGATES

Privity of Contract.front: Alan contracts with BuildCo to construct a summerhouse for £20,000 as a gift for his daughter Bella. BuildCo does substandard work. Alan has suffered no personal financial loss (he did not live there). Bella wants to sue BuildCo directly for the cost of repairs. Can she?
Privity of Contract.answer: Bella faces two issues. First, privity: Bella was not a party to the contract between Alan and BuildCo — under the doctrine of privity (Dunlop v Selfridge [1915]), only parties to a contract can sue on it. Bella cannot sue directly unless the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 applies. Under s.1(1)(b) CRTPA 1999, Bella may enforce the term if it purports to confer a benefit on her and the parties did not intend it to be unenforceable by her. Alan contracted specifically to build the summerhouse as a gift for Bella — this strongly suggests the contract was intended to benefit her. Bella is identified (s.1(3)) and should be able to enforce the building standard under CRTPA 1999. Second, Alan's position: even without CRTPA, Alan can sue for the full cost of remedying defects, even though the financial loss falls on Bella (Linden Gardens Trust v Lenesta Sludge Disposals [1994]: a contracting party can recover substantial damages for third party loss in building contracts). Either route gives a remedy.
Privity of Contract.summary_line: PRIVITY · THIRD PARTY · CRTPA 1999 s1(1)(b) · PURPORTS TO BENEFIT · IDENTIFIED · BELLA CAN ENFORCE · LINDEN GARDENS ALTERNATIVE

Privity of Contract.front: A contract between InsureCo and PolicyHolder expressly states: 'The benefits of this policy may be enforced by any member of the policyholder's immediate family.' The policyholder's son Tom makes a claim under the policy. InsureCo refuses, arguing Tom is not a party to the contract. Can Tom enforce the policy?
Privity of Contract.answer: Yes. Tom can enforce the policy under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. Under s.1(1)(a) CRTPA 1999, a third party may enforce a term where the contract expressly provides that they may do so. The clause here — 'may be enforced by any member of the policyholder's immediate family' — is an express provision within s.1(1)(a). Tom, as the policyholder's son, falls within the class of immediate family members (s.1(3): the third party must be identified by name, class, or description — 'immediate family' is a clear class). InsureCo's argument that Tom is not a party is precisely the kind of privity obstacle that CRTPA 1999 was designed to remove. Tom can enforce the contractual benefit directly against InsureCo. InsureCo may only modify or rescind the policy in accordance with s.2 CRTPA 1999 — it cannot do so unilaterally if Tom has relied on the provision.
Privity of Contract.summary_line: CRTPA 1999 · s1(1)(a) EXPRESS · IMMEDIATE FAMILY CLASS · s1(3) IDENTIFIED · TOM CAN ENFORCE · PRIVITY OVERCOME · s2 VARIATION RESTRICTED

Privity of Contract.front: MusicCo contracts with VenueCo for a series of concerts. MusicCo's manager, acting on her authority, signs the contract on MusicCo's behalf. VenueCo later refuses to perform, arguing the contract was signed by the manager personally, not MusicCo, so MusicCo has no right to sue. Advise MusicCo.
Privity of Contract.answer: MusicCo can sue. The manager contracted as agent for MusicCo (the principal). The fundamental rule of agency is that where an agent contracts on behalf of a disclosed principal with actual or apparent authority, the contract is between the principal and the third party — the agent drops out. MusicCo is the disclosed principal (VenueCo knew they were contracting with MusicCo through its manager) and the manager had actual authority to sign. The privity objection fails — agency is a well-established common law exception to the privity rule (Dunlop v Selfridge [1915]). MusicCo is the contracting party and has full rights to enforce the contract and claim damages for VenueCo's refusal to perform. The manager personally has no rights or liabilities under the contract (absent personal undertakings). VenueCo is bound to perform for MusicCo.
Privity of Contract.summary_line: AGENCY · DISCLOSED PRINCIPAL · ACTUAL AUTHORITY · AGENT DROPS OUT · PRIVITY EXCEPTION · MUSICCO CAN SUE · DUNLOP v SELFRIDGE

Privity of Contract.front: Going deeper: Does the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 adequately solve the privity problem — or does it create new difficulties?
Privity of Contract.answer:
The privity rule — only parties to a contract can sue on it — was established in Tweddle v Atkinson [1861] and confirmed by the HL in Dunlop v Selfridge [1915]. For over a century, courts devised workarounds: collateral contracts (Shanklin Pier v Detel Products [1951]), agency, assignment, and trust. The Law Commission recommended reform in 1996. The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 allows a third party to enforce a contractual term if: (a) the contract expressly provides that they may (s.1(1)(a)); or (b) the term purports to confer a benefit on them (s.1(1)(b)) and they are identified by name, description, or class (s.1(3)). Difficulties the Act creates:

1. S.1
2. allows contracting parties to rebut s.1(1)(b) by indicating the third party was not intended to have enforcement rights — this is a significant loophole that commercial drafters routinely exploit by including boilerplate exclusion clauses.
2. S.2 protects reliant third parties from variation/rescission — but determining when a third party has 'relied' in the relevant sense creates uncertainty.
3. The Act leaves the existing exceptions (collateral contract, agency etc.) intact, meaning practitioners now face two overlapping regimes.
4. The Act has been less influential than expected — many commercial contracts routinely exclude it.

Academic debate: Andrews argues the Act is welcome but incomplete — it does not address the corresponding inability of a third party to have obligations imposed on them. MacMillan notes that international instruments (UNIDROIT, PECL) go further in integrating third party rights into a coherent framework. The privity debate ultimately raises questions about the fundamental nature of contract — as bilateral obligation or as a social institution with wider effects.

Privity of Contract.front: Going deeper: What is the Albazero principle and when can a contracting party recover substantial damages for a third party's loss?
Privity of Contract.answer:
The general rule in contract is that a party can only recover for their own loss — a promisee who suffers no personal loss cannot recover substantial damages even if the promisor's breach causes loss to a third party (Woodar Investment Development v Wimpey [1980]). This creates a 'black hole' of unrecoverable loss in contracts made for the benefit of third parties. The Albazero [1977]: Lord Diplock recognised a narrow exception in the context of shipping contracts. Where parties contract in the knowledge that the cargo will be transferred to a third party who will acquire the risk of loss, the original contracting party (shipper) may sue the carrier for the third party's loss as if it were their own — holding the damages on trust for the third party. The principle was significantly extended in Linden Gardens Trust v Lenesta Sludge [1994] and St Martins Property v Sir Robert McAlpine [1994] (the 'McAlpine cases'): the HL held that in building contracts, where assignment was prohibited and both parties knew the building would be occupied by third parties, the original employer could recover substantial damages for defects even after transferring the property. Panatown v McAlpine [2001]: the HL restricted the principle where the third party had a direct right of action against the contractor (via a Duty of Care Deed) — the black hole argument failed because the loss was recoverable by the third party directly. Academic debate: the Albazero/McAlpine principle creates a pragmatic exception to prevent the privity doctrine from producing the unjust result of entirely unrecoverable loss. McKendrick argues it is now an established but narrow exception, confined to cases where:

1. assignment is prohibited
2. the parties contemplated that a third party would acquire an interest
3. the third party has no direct action.

The CRTPA 1999 has reduced the practical need for the principle but has not abolished it.

Privity of Contract.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A father contracts with a caterer to provide food and service for his daughter Emily's wedding. The contract states: 'Services to be provided for the benefit of Emily Clarke and her guests.' The food is inedible. The father suffered no personal loss. Emily sues the caterer directly.
Privity of Contract.issue: The Trap: Emily was not a party to the contract between her father and the caterer. The privity rule (Tweddle v Atkinson [1861]; Dunlop v Selfridge [1915]) bars third parties from suing on a contract. Students apply privity and conclude Emily has no claim.
Privity of Contract.rule: The Reality: Emily can sue under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. Section 1(1)(b): a third party may enforce a contractual term where it purports to confer a benefit on them and they are identified by name. Emily is named in the contract and the services were expressly for her benefit — both conditions are satisfied.
Privity of Contract.application:
Why the Distractor Fails: The common law privity rule has been substantially modified by the CRTPA 1999. Students who stop at privity miss the statutory exception. The Act creates a right of action where:

1. the contract expressly provides the third party may enforce it (s.1(1)(a)), OR
2. the term purports to confer a benefit on them and they are identified by name, class, or description (s.1(1)(b)).

Emily is named; the services were for her benefit. The caterer cannot rely on privity to escape liability to the very person the contract was designed to benefit.
Privity of Contract.conclusion: The Key Rule: CRTPA 1999 s.1 — a named third party beneficiary may enforce a term that purports to confer a benefit on them. Always check the Act before concluding a third party has no claim. Privity is a common law rule; the 1999 Act is a statutory override that applies in the majority of third party beneficiary situations.
Privity of Contract.summary_line: PRIVITY · CRTPA 1999 s1 · NAMED THIRD PARTY · PURPORTS TO CONFER BENEFIT · TWEDDLE · DUNLOP · STATUTORY OVERRIDE
